events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Detained immigrants in New Jersey enduring reported inhumane conditions including inadequate food and medical care

events.title: Armed Conflict in Tyre
events.body:
Homes in the Lebanese city of Tyre were struck by Israeli bombs shortly after residents were ordered to evacuate the area. The strikes destroyed houses, leaving families with little more than rubble.

Despite the destruction and evacuation orders, many residents are refusing to leave. Some say they intend to stay and live among the ruins of what were their homes.

The situation leaves civilians in a difficult position — caught between military orders to go and a determination to remain in the only places they have known.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Lebanon displaced or sheltering in rubble after homes destroyed by airstrikes

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Guatemala
events.body:
Guatemala's government publicly denied reports on Thursday that it had agreed to allow the United States to carry out military strikes against drug traffickers on its soil. Officials stated clearly that no agreement authorizing foreign military operations within the country exists, pushing back against a news report citing anonymous sources that said the president had approved such action.

At the same time, Guatemala confirmed it had written to U.S. defense officials requesting joint military operations led by Guatemalan forces, with U.S. assistance, against drug groups. The government stressed this request was made under existing bilateral agreements and within the bounds of its constitution — not an open invitation for unilateral U.S. strikes.

The dispute comes amid broader tension across Latin America over U.S. military activity in the region. Guatemala has faced serious gang violence tied to the drug trade, including an attack earlier this year in which at least 10 police officers were killed. Many regional governments have said they welcome security cooperation but not direct foreign military intervention.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Guatemala navigating tension over foreign military involvement amid serious drug cartel violence

events.title: Civil Unrest in Dublin
events.body:
A football friendly between the Republic of Ireland and Qatar in Dublin was stopped twice by protesters in the stands. Fans threw tennis balls covered with Palestinian flag images onto the field at roughly 10 and 20 minutes into the match, halting play each time. The protests were directed at Ireland's upcoming fixtures against Israel in the UEFA Nations League, scheduled for late September and early October.

Pressure over the fixtures has been building for months. Ireland's parliament saw separate protests on the day before the match, and the Football Association of Ireland passed a motion last November asking European football's governing body to suspend Israel from competition — a request that received no support. The national team manager has publicly called for Israel to be banned from international football.

Despite the protests, football officials say both Israel matches will go ahead as scheduled. Ireland won the Qatar friendly 1-0.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Irish football fans and officials amid intense public pressure over upcoming matches involving Israel

events.title: Civil Unrest in Newark
events.body:
Protesters have gathered outside the Delaney Hall immigration detention facility near Newark, New Jersey, following reports that detainees inside are staging a hunger strike. The facility, run by a private contractor under a federal immigration enforcement contract, was reopened in February 2025. Local officials have questioned whether it was properly permitted, and critics have raised concerns about conditions inside.

On Wednesday night, demonstrators attempted to block access to the facility. Some wore gas masks and formed human chains using makeshift barriers. Federal authorities said six protesters were arrested for allegedly assaulting federal agents.

Elected officials who have toured the facility described serious concerns, including spoiled food and people unable to see a doctor for over a week. State health inspectors were reportedly denied full access during an inspection visit. A CNN report cited in the source found that 50 immigrants have died in detention nationwide during the current administration — the highest number in at least two decades.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Detainees and protesters in New Jersey amid reported hunger strikes, denied medical care, and disputed detention conditions

events.title: Armed Conflict in Ukraine
events.body:
Nearly 500,000 Russian soldiers have been killed since Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, according to the head of the UK's intelligence agency GCHQ. The figure was shared in a public speech marking the official's first major address in the role. Ukraine's president separately said earlier this year that Ukraine had lost 55,000 soldiers since the war began.

Independent verification efforts by a team of journalists and volunteers have confirmed the names of more than 223,000 Russian soldiers killed, using obituaries, social media, and cemetery records. Analysts believe those confirmed deaths represent only 45 to 65 percent of the actual toll, meaning the real number could be significantly higher.

The war, now entering its fourth year, continues to cause enormous loss of life on both sides. Casualty figures from both governments have been limited and contested throughout the conflict.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Hundreds of thousands of soldiers killed on both sides as war in Ukraine enters its fourth year

events.title: Displacement in Johannesburg
events.body:
Ghana's government chartered a flight to bring nearly 300 of its citizens home from South Africa after protests against illegal immigration raised fears of a return to xenophobic violence. The passengers landed in Accra, Ghana's capital, on Wednesday. Around 800 Ghanaians registered for repatriation, and more departures are expected in the coming days.

The protests, organized by a group calling itself March and March, have spread from Durban to other provinces, and the group has set a June 30 deadline for undocumented migrants to leave South Africa. Past waves of anti-foreigner violence killed at least 12 people in 2019 and 62 in 2008. Protest organizers say recent demonstrations have been peaceful, but many foreign nationals say they feel threatened.

More than 300 migrants, including Congolese nationals, have been sheltering outside South Africa's home affairs office in Durban, saying they fear they will be killed if they remain after the deadline. Ghana's government says it will help returning citizens reintegrate and restart the businesses they operated in South Africa.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Ghanaian and other migrants in South Africa facing threats, displacement, and fear of xenophobic violence

events.title: Armed Conflict in Tyre
events.body:
Israel's military has issued its largest evacuation order since a ceasefire took effect last month, telling residents across roughly 14% of Lebanese territory to move north of the Zahrani River. The order covers about 300 towns and villages and came alongside airstrikes on Tyre, one of the largest cities in southern Lebanon. Residents described watching strikes hit their city from their balconies, and many began packing and leaving for the port and other gathering points. Four people were reported killed Wednesday in the towns of Choukine and Nabatieh.

The escalation follows a deadly 24-hour period in which more than 150 Israeli strikes hit about 50 towns and villages. Lebanon's health ministry said at least 31 people died on Tuesday alone, including 15 in Burj al-Shamali.

Humanitarian workers warned that the coastal city of Sidon can no longer absorb more displaced families, urging people to head to the Beqaa Valley or Mount Lebanon. Many already-displaced residents have no clear place to go.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese civilians displaced again amid renewed airstrikes and overcrowded shelters

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kyiv
events.body:
One of the largest Russian bombardments of Kyiv in the four-year war struck the Ukrainian capital on Sunday, hitting hospitals, schools, and a television studio. Russia then announced plans for further "systematic" strikes targeting what it described as defense companies and military sites in the city, and urged foreign diplomats to leave for their own safety.

The European Union, Germany, and dozens of other countries pushed back sharply. Germany summoned Russia's ambassador, and the EU called in Russia's charge d'affaires in Brussels. Both said they would not be evacuating their Kyiv missions. A joint statement signed by 50 countries, read at the United Nations, condemned the attacks and the threats to embassies.

The UN secretary-general warned that the announced campaign risked prolonging civilian suffering and pushing peace further out of reach. He noted that global conflicts are currently at their highest number since the UN was founded, with Ukraine among the most severe.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: for civilians in Kyiv enduring ongoing bombardment, and for meaningful steps toward peace

events.title: Rasht
events.body:
Iranians are facing growing difficulty finding and affording basic medicines. Sanctions, banking restrictions, and currency instability had already strained the country's pharmaceutical system for years. The recent conflict with the US and Israel has added new pressure by disrupting regional supply routes, damaging parts of Iran's health infrastructure, and raising transport costs.

The shortages are no longer limited to rare drugs — routine medicines are now affected. Patients are traveling between multiple pharmacies without success. In the northern city of Rasht, insulin was reportedly being rationed and sold at six times its previous price. One person managing a chronic condition said he has only 18 days of medication left after six weeks of searching pharmacy to pharmacy. A cardiologist noted that some patients are simply stopping treatment because they cannot afford the cost.

Informal networks that once allowed families to receive medicines sent from abroad are also becoming harder to use. Some patients now rely on social media groups to find out which pharmacies still have stock.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: for iranian patients struggling to find or afford essential medicines, and for relief workers seeking to meet urgent humanitarian needs

events.title: Democratic Republic of the Congo
events.body:
A fast-moving Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo has surpassed 900 suspected cases, with at least 220 suspected deaths, according to the head of the World Health Organization. The strain involved is the Bundibugyo type of Ebola, and officials say the epidemic is spreading faster than containment efforts can keep up.

The World Health Organization's leader made the announcement on a Monday, though the exact date was not specified in the report. The outbreak is described as rapidly outpacing the response, raising serious concern among global health authorities.

Ebola spreads through direct contact with bodily fluids of infected people and can be fatal without prompt medical care. Communities in affected areas face disrupted daily life, fear of infection, and strain on local health systems already stretched thin.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Communities in the Democratic Republic of the Congo facing a fast-spreading Ebola outbreak with over 900 cases and 220 deaths

events.title: Armed Conflict in Dnipro
events.body:
A Russian attack hit a warehouse in Dnipro, Ukraine, destroying a large amount of food aid that had been set aside for people living in frontline areas. The World Food Programme condemned the strike and described the loss as significant.

The destroyed supplies had been intended to reach thousands of people who rely on humanitarian assistance to survive amid ongoing fighting. The attack leaves aid organizations scrambling to meet those needs through other means.

Strikes on humanitarian infrastructure cut off vital support for civilians who have few other options for food. The loss adds pressure to aid efforts already stretched thin by the demands of a prolonged conflict.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Civilians across Ukraine losing critical food aid after strikes destroy humanitarian supply warehouses

events.title: Armed Conflict in Lebanon
events.body:
Overnight airstrikes in Lebanon forced residents to flee their homes again, according to a United Nations report. The strikes were described as intensified, adding to ongoing displacement pressure in the region.

In the Gaza Strip, aid organizations continue to face serious obstacles getting humanitarian supplies into the area. The United Nations reported that restrictions on aid access remain in place, limiting what relief workers can deliver to civilians inside the enclave.

Both situations are creating hardship for ordinary people — those losing their homes to airstrikes in Lebanon and those cut off from food, medicine, and other essentials in Gaza.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: for families forced to flee their homes in Lebanon, and for aid to reach civilians in Gaza who are cut off from basic necessities

events.title: Armed Conflict in Darfur
events.body:
The United Nations has raised alarm over ongoing drone strikes in Sudan's Darfur region. The attacks have reportedly killed civilians and deepened fear among communities already living through a brutal conflict between two rival military forces.

The UN's warning reflects growing concern that civilians in Darfur have little safety or escape from the fighting. The region has long been one of the most dangerous places in Sudan, and the addition of drone strikes adds another layer of danger for people caught between the warring sides.

No specific death toll was reported, but the strikes are intensifying an already dire situation for residents in the area.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Civilians in Sudan caught between rival military forces amid intensifying drone strikes and deepening fear

events.title: Displacement in eastern Congo
events.body:
The World Health Organization has warned that eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo is facing what it calls a "catastrophic collision of disease and conflict." A fast-spreading Ebola outbreak is outpacing efforts to contain it in a region already torn apart by armed violence and mass displacement.

The situation is made worse by acute hunger affecting the same population. The combination of active conflict, large numbers of displaced people, and food insecurity makes it extremely difficult for health teams to track and stop the spread of the disease.

WHO issued the warning on Wednesday, signaling that conditions on the ground are deteriorating faster than response efforts can keep up. Civilians in the region are now facing multiple overlapping crises at the same time, leaving communities with little capacity to cope.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Displaced civilians in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo amid overlapping Ebola outbreak, armed conflict, and acute hunger

events.title: Armed Conflict in Nabatieh
events.body:
Israeli forces have ordered the evacuation of Nabatieh, one of the largest cities in southern Lebanon, and carried out what reporters described as near-continuous artillery shelling of towns on the city's outskirts. Air strikes also hit a cemetery within the city limits, and the nearby village of Yohmor al-Shaqif was struck multiple times. The attacks came during the Eid al-Adha holiday. Hezbollah responded with artillery and drone strikes on Israeli forces near the town of Zawtar al-Sharqiya.

Israeli forces have killed at least 3,213 people in Lebanon, including paramedics and civilians, since intensifying their campaign in March. A ceasefire announced in April has not held, and Israel has established a buffer zone in the south while expanding operations beyond what it calls a "yellow line."

Nabatieh sits just north of the Litani River and serves as a major economic and administrative center for southern Lebanon. Analysts quoted in the source say the city is being targeted in part to put pressure on the broader Shia community and make daily life and return impossible for displaced residents.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Lebanon facing ongoing airstrikes, forced evacuations, and mounting casualties during a major religious holiday

events.title: Armed Conflict in Gaza City
events.body:
An Israeli airstrike in a busy area of Gaza City killed a man believed to be the leader of Hamas's military wing. Israel said the strike was deliberate, and the man's family confirmed his death, though Hamas has made no official statement.

A funeral was held for the man following the strike. The fact that the strike took place in a crowded area raises concern for bystanders and residents nearby.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: for the families grieving losses and for all civilians in Gaza City caught in ongoing strikes

events.title: Shanxi
events.body:
At least 82 people were killed and two others remain missing after a gas explosion tore through a coal mine in Shanxi province, northern China. Surveillance footage broadcast by state media captured the moment of the blast inside the Liushenyu Coal Mine. Officials reported that carbon monoxide levels in the mine had exceeded safe limits, though the full cause of the explosion has not been determined.

The disaster is described as the worst mining accident in China since 2009. The country's top leader called for no effort to be spared in search and rescue operations. The two workers still unaccounted for leave families waiting for news as crews continue working at the site.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Families in China grieving or awaiting news after a deadly coal mine explosion killed dozens

events.title: Civil Unrest in Barinas
events.body:
Violent clashes broke out between inmates and security personnel at a prison called Injuba in the Venezuelan state of Barinas. Prisoners climbed onto the roof and burned mattresses to protest what they described as mistreatment under a new prison director, including violent searches, solitary confinement, and general abuse. Security forces were deployed to the facility, and inmates reported being shot at. Videos shared by a prisoner rights group showed wounds on inmates and protesters chanting "we want justice."

Inmates also raised concerns about a lack of medicine for prisoners with tuberculosis. Neither the prison director nor Venezuela's interim government has publicly responded. The Venezuelan Prison Observatory, which recorded and shared footage of the protest, has long warned that many of the country's jails fail to meet basic legal standards. A UN human rights official noted earlier this year that reports of torture in Venezuelan detention facilities had continued even after a change in national leadership.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Inmates in Venezuela protesting abuse, lack of medicine, and violent conditions inside overcrowded prisons

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Phnom Penh
events.body:
Cambodia's former opposition leader, who had been serving a 27-year sentence on treason charges, has been pardoned by the country's acting head of state. The pardon was signed by the former prime minister on behalf of the king, who is currently receiving medical treatment abroad. The opposition figure had been under house arrest since being convicted in 2023, following his initial arrest in 2017.

Human rights groups have long called the charges politically motivated. The former opposition leader was arrested less than a year before a crucial election, and his party was later banned from contesting it, leaving the country effectively under one-party rule.

While the pardon ends the sentence, it does not restore full freedoms. The pardoned politician remains barred from leaving Cambodia for five years and cannot participate in politics. Human rights observers say other opposition figures in the country still face the threat of arrest and restrictions, and called on the government to respect political rights more broadly.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Pardoned opposition leader in Cambodia still barred from politics amid broader restrictions on political rights

events.title: Murchison Falls National Park
events.body:
Three people died and four others were injured when their vehicle struck an elephant inside Murchison Falls National Park in northwestern Uganda on Sunday evening. The vehicle was carrying seven government tax authority officials who were traveling from Arua city to the capital, Kampala, when the collision occurred.

The injured were taken first to a nearby hospital and then transferred to Kampala for further treatment. No information was provided about the condition of the elephant.

Wildlife officials say incidents involving animals and vehicles are becoming more frequent as communities expand into areas bordering protected wildlife zones. Uganda's Wildlife Authority reminded drivers traveling through national parks and protected areas to slow down and watch for animals crossing the road.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Seven Ugandan tax officials struck by an elephant in a national park, three killed and four injured

events.title: Armed Conflict in Bekaa Valley
events.body:
Israel's military has launched a new wave of strikes across Lebanon, including in the Bekaa Valley in the eastern part of the country. The Israeli prime minister announced the strikes publicly, saying Israel is "at war with Hezbollah" and that he had ordered the military to "deal them a crushing blow" and increase the intensity of attacks. The expansion into the Bekaa Valley marks a geographic widening of Israel's campaign beyond the southern areas where fighting has been concentrated since a ceasefire was agreed in April.

More than 400 people in Lebanon have been killed since that ceasefire went into effect, including many paramedics and emergency workers. Israel has continued to issue evacuation orders in southern Lebanon, adding to over one million people already displaced. Lebanon's government says it is working to disarm Hezbollah but argues a ceasefire is needed to complete the process. Residents in Beirut are concerned that the latest escalation could bring strikes to the capital.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians across Lebanon facing escalating airstrikes, mass displacement, and over 400 deaths since ceasefire

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kyiv
events.body:
Russia has threatened fresh "systematic strikes" on Kyiv, days after launching one of the largest aerial attacks on the Ukrainian capital since the war began. The strikes over Saturday night killed four people and injured around 100. Dozens of ballistic and cruise missiles, hundreds of drones, and a hypersonic Oreshnik missile were used in the assault. Non-military sites hit or damaged included a museum dedicated to Chernobyl, the National Art Museum of Ukraine, a shopping centre, a market, and several residential buildings.

Russia says the attacks are in response to a Ukrainian strike on a student dormitory in Starobilsk that Russian officials say killed 21 people. Ukraine says it targeted a military drone unit in the area, not civilians.

Russia has now called on foreign nationals and diplomats to leave Kyiv as soon as possible, warning them to stay away from administrative and military buildings. Many observers view this warning as psychological pressure rather than a sign of any new phase in the conflict.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians in Ukraine facing mass aerial bombardment with missiles and drones targeting residential and cultural sites

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kyiv
events.body:
Russia launched a large overnight missile and drone attack on Ukraine, targeting the capital, Kyiv. The strike reportedly involved around 90 missiles — including a powerful hypersonic ballistic missile — and about 60 drones.

The scale of the attack drew a response from the United Nations' top official in Ukraine, who called for an end to harm against civilians. Residents described the fear and uncertainty of the attack, with one person expressing hope that friends had survived.

Attacks of this size cause widespread disruption, damage to homes and infrastructure, and civilian casualties. People in Kyiv and surrounding areas faced another night of danger as the conflict continues with no sign of an immediate end.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Civilians across Ukraine facing large-scale overnight missile and drone strikes causing casualties and widespread destruction

events.title: Displacement in Gaza
events.body:
A woman described life in a crowded displacement camp in Gaza, where she lives in a simple tent that provides little protection from cold or heat. She spoke with UN News about the contrast between her life before the war and what it has become.

The camp she lives in is packed with tents that offer residents almost no privacy and very limited access to basic services. Her account reflects the situation faced by large numbers of displaced people across Gaza who have lost their homes since the conflict began.

Conditions in such camps remain extremely difficult, with families enduring hardship day to day with minimal shelter and support.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: for families living in overcrowded displacement camps in Gaza, who have lost their homes and are struggling without basic shelter, privacy, or services

events.title: Ituri
events.body:
An Ebola outbreak centered in the northeastern Congolese province of Ituri has now killed an estimated 220 people, with more than 900 suspected cases identified. The World Health Organization declared the outbreak a public health emergency of international concern last week, warning that the epidemic is "outpacing" responders. The strain involved is Bundibugyo, for which no vaccine or approved treatment currently exists. The outbreak has spread as far as 200 kilometers from its starting point and has crossed into Uganda, where seven confirmed cases have been reported, including two health workers at a private facility in the capital, Kampala.

Tensions around the response have turned violent in the DRC. On Sunday evening, armed men stormed a hospital in Mongbwalu treating Ebola patients, demanding the bodies of deceased relatives. Gunfire broke out and staff worked to evacuate patients. The day before, residents set fire to a treatment tent run by a humanitarian group, and 18 suspected Ebola patients left and were unaccounted for. A treatment center in Rwampara was also burned after families were blocked from retrieving a body.

Authorities have banned funeral gatherings of more than 50 people in the northeast, a rule that has sparked further resistance from grieving communities.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Ebola outbreak in Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda killing hundreds with no available vaccine or treatment

events.title: Civil Unrest in international waters (Mediterranean)
events.body:
Eleven Australians were among roughly 430 volunteers on a flotilla intercepted by Israeli forces in international waters last week as the group attempted to deliver aid to Gaza. Israeli forces stopped the boats and detained those on board, bringing them to the port of Ashdod.

Several Australian activists have since returned home and described what they say was serious mistreatment during four days in Israeli custody. One woman said she was dragged, sexually assaulted, and beaten. A man reported a fractured rib along with bruises and cuts, and said he also saw others struck with rubber bullets, Tasered, and subjected to stun grenades. Flotilla organisers say at least 15 people reported incidents of sexual assault or rape while detained.

The incident has drawn international reaction. Malaysia said it is preparing legal action over the alleged treatment of its nationals. Foreign ministers from eight countries jointly condemned what they called degrading treatment of detainees. France announced it would bar a senior Israeli official who publicly posted footage mocking the kneeling, blindfolded detainees.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: for the detained activists dealing with trauma and injury, and for humanitarian aid to reach civilians in Gaza

events.title: Armed Conflict in Quetta
events.body:
A suicide car bomb struck a train carrying soldiers in Quetta, the capital of Pakistan's Balochistan province, killing at least 24 people and wounding more than 50 others. The blast overturned and set fire to train carriages and heavily damaged nearby homes and buildings. Hospitals in Quetta declared a state of emergency and ordered all medical staff to remain on duty.

An armed separatist group called the Balochistan Liberation Army claimed responsibility. The group seeks an independent state and opposes large-scale Chinese investment in the region, including infrastructure projects linking China's Xinjiang region to Balochistan's Gwadar port.

The attack is part of a sharp rise in violence across Balochistan. Research shows the province recorded at least 254 attacks in 2025, about 26 percent more than in 2024, while bomb and grenade attacks grew by more than 65 percent in the first eleven months of 2025 compared to the same period the year before.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Soldiers, civilians, and medical workers in Pakistan amid a deadly suicide bombing and rising separatist violence

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kyiv
events.body:
Russia has announced plans for "systematic strikes" on defense-related facilities in Kyiv and urged foreign citizens, including embassy staff, to leave the Ukrainian capital. The Russian Foreign Ministry said the planned strikes are a response to a Ukrainian drone attack that hit a student dormitory in Starobilsk, in Russian-occupied Luhansk, killing at least 18 people and wounding 42 more. Ukraine's military denied targeting civilians, saying it struck a drone command unit at that location.

The threat comes amid an ongoing wave of Russian missile and drone attacks on Kyiv. At least four people were killed and more than 60 injured in overnight strikes on the capital and surrounding areas. Russia confirmed use of an Oreshnik hypersonic ballistic missile — the third time that nuclear-capable weapon has been used in the war. Strikes also killed several people in the Kharkiv and Donetsk regions.

Ukraine's foreign minister urged allies not to yield to what he called "Russian blackmail," while more than 70 foreign diplomats visited a damaged Kyiv neighborhood to pay respects to recent victims.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Ukraine facing escalating missile and drone strikes on Kyiv and multiple regions

events.title: Civil Unrest in Tunis
events.body:
A Tunisian lawyer and public commentator has been sentenced to two years in prison for criticizing prison conditions in a 2023 radio interview. A court in Tunis handed down the verdict following a complaint filed by the country's General Administration of Prisons. Her lawyer has lodged an appeal.

This is not her first conviction. She was previously sentenced to 18 months for a televised comment in May 2024, and an appeal court added another 18 months for criticizing racially segregated cemeteries and bus services. She was arrested by masked officers in May 2024, held for over 18 months, and released on conditional parole last November. She now faces prosecution in five separate cases.

All charges are tied to public statements and brought under a 2022 "false information" law that human rights groups say is used to silence critics. Lawyers, journalists, and activists have increasingly faced prosecution under this law or on anti-terrorism charges since the president consolidated power in 2021.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Lawyers, journalists, and activists in Tunisia facing prosecution and imprisonment for public criticism and free expression

events.title: Armed Conflict in southern Lebanon
events.body:
Lebanon observed Liberation Day on May 25, a holiday marking the date in 2000 when Israel ended a 22-year occupation of the country's south. This year's anniversary took on a heavy tone as people gathered to commemorate the occasion while Israeli bombardment continued in the region.

Reporting from Lebanon described the contrast between the meaning of the holiday and current conditions on the ground, noting that occupation has returned to the south. Residents are once again living through a period of military activity in the same areas that the holiday was meant to celebrate as free.

The situation leaves civilians marking a day of remembered liberation while facing renewed conflict in their communities.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Lebanon marking liberation while enduring renewed military bombardment in the south

events.title: Armed Conflict in Khan Younis
events.body:
A six-year-old girl was killed by an Israeli airstrike on a displacement camp in Khan Younis while she was playing at the door of her family's home. A woman was also killed in the same strike. Several other children and a baby were injured in the attack.

The strike hit an area where displaced families were sheltering. Civilians, including very young children, continue to be among those killed and wounded in ongoing fighting in Gaza.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: for the family of the six-year-old girl killed, and for all children living in displacement camps who face daily danger

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kyiv
events.body:
An overnight Russian missile strike hit Kyiv, destroying nearly half of the exhibits at the National Chornobyl Museum, according to officials. The museum preserves history and memory related to the 1986 Chornobyl nuclear disaster.

Russian officials have threatened systematic and widespread attacks on Ukraine's capital. The strike on the museum adds to growing concern about civilian infrastructure being hit in the ongoing conflict.

Residents of Kyiv continue to face the threat of aerial attacks with little warning, and cultural and historical sites have not been spared.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Residents of Kyiv facing ongoing aerial strikes and destruction of irreplaceable cultural and historical sites in Ukraine

events.title: Armed Conflict in Starobilsk
events.body:
A strike on a college dormitory in Starobilsk, a town in Russian-occupied eastern Ukraine, killed 21 people and injured 42 others in the early hours of Friday, May 21. Russian officials accused Ukraine of carrying out a drone attack on the building, which housed students. Images showed the five-storey structure reduced to rubble, with rescue teams searching the debris. The operation concluded late Saturday.

Russia called an emergency session of the United Nations Security Council and its ambassador called the strike a war crime under international humanitarian law. Ukraine's military confirmed it carried out an attack near Starobilsk that night but said it targeted a Russian military unit, not the dormitory.

Russia's president ordered the defense ministry to prepare a response. Some pro-Kremlin commentators publicly called for retaliatory strikes beyond Ukraine, including against European countries, raising concern about potential escalation beyond Ukrainian territory.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians in Russian-occupied eastern Ukraine killed and injured after a deadly strike on a college dormitory

events.title: Armed Conflict in Strait of Hormuz
events.body:
A young Indian sailor on his first sea job survived a missile strike on an oil tanker in the Strait of Hormuz in the early hours of March 1. The vessel, the Skylight, was struck near one of the world's busiest shipping routes after traveling from Dubai. The missile hit the engine room, starting a fire that spread quickly through the ship and cut off all power. Survivors jumped into the sea and were rescued within an hour by the Omani Navy.

Two crew members were killed — the ship's captain and a 25-year-old man from Rajasthan, India, who had been working in the engine room at the time of the strike. His body has never been found.

The attack was not isolated. According to maritime data, 38 commercial vessels have been struck in and around the Strait since the conflict began. More than 20,000 seafarers are currently trapped in the Gulf, with thousands reporting unpaid wages, food and water shortages, and no clear way home.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Over 20,000 seafarers trapped in the Gulf facing unpaid wages, shortages, and danger from ongoing vessel strikes

events.title: Shanxi province
events.body:
At least 82 people were killed and two remain missing after a gas explosion at a coal mine in Shanxi province in northern China. The blast happened on Friday evening, when about 247 workers were on duty. More than 100 people were rescued, and 128 are being treated in hospitals, including two in critical condition. Most were affected by inhaling poisonous gas.

Rescue teams of hundreds of workers were sent to the site, and robots with gas sensors and cameras were deployed underground to reach areas too dangerous for workers. Recovery efforts have been complicated by water buildup near the blast site and by mine maps that did not match the actual layout underground.

The mine had been flagged as a serious safety hazard in 2024, and the company that operates it received two safety penalties earlier this year. Authorities have ordered immediate safety inspections across Shanxi province and suspended operations at all four mines run by the same group.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Dozens of coal mine workers in China killed or hospitalized after a deadly underground gas explosion

events.title: Alcacer do Sal
events.body:
Two young boys, ages four and five, were found crying beside a road near Alcacer do Sal in southern Portugal on a Tuesday evening after allegedly being abandoned there by their mother and her partner. A witness reported that one of the boys said they had been blindfolded and told to look for a hidden toy — and when they removed their blindfolds, their mother was gone.

The boys' French mother and her partner were arrested two days later in Fatima and brought before a judge, who ordered them held in pre-trial detention. Both face charges of child endangerment and abandonment; the man faces an additional charge of aggravated assault.

The children, who had been living with their mother in Colmar, France, are now with a foster family in Lisbon and are expected to return to France. Their father had reported them missing on May 11, which triggered a search and a European arrest warrant.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Two young boys abandoned roadside in Portugal now in foster care awaiting family reunification

events.title: Garden Grove
events.body:
Thousands of residents in Garden Grove, California, have been forced to leave their homes after officials warned that a tank holding about 7,000 gallons of a highly flammable chemical at a nearby manufacturing facility was at serious risk of rupturing or exploding. The chemical, used in plastics manufacturing, can cause breathing problems, eye irritation, and dizziness if inhaled. California's governor declared a state of emergency in response.

Emergency crews have been spraying the tank with water to try to keep temperatures down, but officials discovered the internal temperature was far higher than outside readings suggested. Inspectors found a possible crack in the tank, which may be releasing some pressure. An inoperable valve has added to the difficulty of managing the situation.

Local schools have closed, several road exits have been shut, and officials are building barriers to contain any chemical spill and keep it from reaching storm drains or the ocean. Residents were told that anyone smelling a fruity or heavy odor should contact authorities immediately.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Thousands of California residents evacuated due to a dangerous chemical tank at risk of rupturing

events.title: Angeles
events.body:
A nine-story building under construction collapsed in the early morning hours in Angeles, a city about 90 kilometers northwest of Manila. At least one person was killed — an older man from Malaysia who had been staying in a nearby hotel struck by falling debris. He was able to reach rescuers by phone while pinned under a wall, but had died by the time they reached him.

Around 24 people have been rescued from the main collapse site and two more from the hotel. Officials believe roughly 20 people may still be trapped, many of them construction workers who could not escape in time. Rescuers say large chunks of concrete are making the work slow and difficult.

Authorities are investigating the cause. The collapse sent scaffolding and concrete spilling into the street. An eyewitness said she heard loud rumbling before losing consciousness briefly and waking to see debris covering two streets.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Dozens of workers and bystanders in the Philippines trapped or killed after a nine-story building collapsed

events.title: Cassowary Coast
events.body:
A man died after a shark attack off the Cassowary Coast in Queensland, a stretch of coastline between Cairns and Townsville. He and three companions had taken a private boat to Kennedy Shoal, a shallow reef about 45 kilometers offshore, where he was believed to be spearfishing when the attack occurred. One of the others in the water at the time pulled him out, but he was declared dead when they reached shore roughly an hour later.

Police described the death as sudden but not suspicious and said they are preparing a report for the coroner. Authorities noted they are offering support to the three companions who witnessed the attack.

This is the second fatal shark attack in Australian waters within two weeks. A 38-year-old was also killed while spearfishing near Perth the previous Saturday. Shark encounters along Australia's coastline are relatively common, though most are not fatal.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: A man's companions in Australia traumatized after witnessing a fatal shark attack while spearfishing offshore

events.title: Civil Unrest in Ankara
events.body:
Riot police forced their way into the headquarters of Turkey's main opposition party in Ankara on Sunday, using tear gas to disperse party members who had barricaded the entrances. Those inside threw objects at officers and sprayed them with hoses. The standoff followed a court ruling earlier in the week that removed the party's elected leader and replaced the entire executive with figures aligned with a previous leadership.

The ousted leader appeared outside the building afterward, telling supporters that the party would take its fight to the streets and public squares. His rival's representatives had asked police to enforce the court's decision after being denied entry to the building.

Human rights observers warned the day before the raid that the government was using damaging tactics against the opposition. The court ruling also means the party's current decisions may no longer be recognized, leaving its future direction deeply uncertain.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Opposition party members and supporters in Turkey amid a court-ordered leadership removal and violent police raids

events.title: Armed Conflict in Quetta
events.body:
At least 20 people were killed and 70 others wounded when an explosion tore through a train passing through Chaman Phatak station in Quetta, the capital of Pakistan's Balochistan region, on Sunday morning. Three coaches and the engine were derailed, and two coaches overturned. The train was carrying military personnel and their families who were heading home for the Eid holiday. Among the dead were three soldiers.

A separatist group called the Balochistan Liberation Army claimed responsibility, saying it was a suicide bombing carried out with a vehicle packed with explosives. Pakistani officials confirmed the attack but had not yet verified the suicide bombing claim. Hospitals declared emergencies to handle the large number of casualties.

This is not the first attack on trains in the region. The same group has targeted trains repeatedly over the past two years, including a hostage-taking incident in March 2025. Residents near the blast site said the explosion shattered windows in nearby homes.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Families and military personnel in Pakistan killed and wounded in a deadly train bombing during Eid holiday travel

events.title: Shanxi
events.body:
At least 82 people were killed and two others remain missing after a gas explosion at the Liushenyu Coal Mine in Shanxi province, northern China. Officials described it as the deadliest mining disaster in the country since 2009.

The country's president stated that no effort should be spared in the search and rescue operation. Early on Sunday morning, rescue teams sent mine inspection robots underground equipped with gas sensors and infrared cameras to help locate survivors.

Journalists from international news outlets, including a BBC correspondent, traveled to the scene of the blast. The cause of the gas explosion has not been detailed beyond the initial reports from officials and state media.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Families in China grieving after a deadly coal mine gas explosion killed at least 82 workers

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kyiv
events.body:
Russia launched a large overnight wave of strikes against Ukraine, firing around 90 missiles and 600 drones beginning Saturday evening. Four people were killed in Kyiv and the surrounding region, and roughly 100 people were injured across the country — including 69 in the capital alone. Residential buildings, shopping centers, a school, an opera house, and the Chornobyl Museum in Kyiv were among the sites damaged. A water-supply facility was also struck.

Russia's defense ministry confirmed using the Oreshnik hypersonic missile, which reportedly travels at more than ten times the speed of sound and is difficult to intercept. It said the strikes targeted military command posts, not civilian infrastructure. Ukraine's military said it shot down or intercepted the majority of incoming weapons but acknowledged 16 direct missile hits and 51 drone hits across 54 locations.

European leaders condemned the attack. Russia described its strikes as a response to Ukrainian attacks on what it called civilian targets. Ordinary residents spent the night in bomb shelters as explosions were heard across Kyiv.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians across Ukraine enduring mass overnight missile and drone strikes killing and injuring hundreds

events.title: Civil Unrest in Johannesburg
events.body:
Attacks on migrants are resurfacing in parts of South Africa, and critics say the government's response is failing to prevent violence or hold perpetrators accountable. Migrants from Zimbabwe, Nigeria, Somalia, Ethiopia, and other African countries have faced repeated outbreaks of violence over many years. More than 60 people were killed in anti-immigrant riots in 2008, and armed mobs attacked foreign-owned businesses around Johannesburg in 2019, killing at least 12 people.

Officials have condemned the violence and warned civilians against enforcing immigration laws on their own. However, advocacy groups say some official responses have made things worse, including reports that people seeking protection during unrest were dispersed with rubber bullets.

A civilian movement calling for mass deportations of undocumented migrants has grown in influence, while researchers argue that migrants are being blamed for unemployment and crime they did not cause. For many migrants, fear has become part of everyday life.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Migrants across South Africa facing renewed violence, displacement, and fear amid rising anti-immigrant unrest

events.title: Civil Unrest in Belgrade
events.body:
Tens of thousands of people rallied in Belgrade's Slavija Square on Saturday, with a student-led movement demanding early elections and an end to what they describe as corruption and the erosion of democratic institutions. The protest movement began about 18 months ago after a concrete canopy collapsed at a railway station in Novi Sad, killing 16 people. Critics blamed the tragedy on poor workmanship and corruption tied to a renovation project.

While most of the rally was peaceful, some groups later clashed with police, throwing flares, rocks, and bottles. Riot officers responded with tear gas and stun grenades. Trains into and out of Belgrade were also canceled, with organizers accusing the state rail operator of trying to block people from traveling to the protest.

A European human rights official raised concerns the day before about increasing attacks on journalists and activists in Serbia. Those who attended the rally risk being labeled as foreign agents or terrorists by pro-government voices.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Tens of thousands in Serbia rallying amid clashes, press freedom concerns, and demands for democratic accountability

events.title: Displacement in Zaporizhzhia
events.body:
Russian authorities have set a July 1, 2026 deadline for property owners in Russian-occupied parts of Ukraine to re-register their land and homes under Russian law. To do so, owners must appear in person and show a Russian passport — something most displaced Ukrainians cannot or will not do. Russian officials have already labeled around 550,000 properties in occupied territories as "ownerless," including 13,000 homes in Mariupol alone. These properties can now be transferred to Russian citizens.

For many Ukrainians who fled the occupation, returning is not a real option. Human rights workers warn that traveling to occupied areas through the only available crossing — Moscow's Sheremetyevo airport — exposes people to unpredictable screening by Russian officials. In the best case, a person could be turned away; in the worst case, arrested.

One woman who fled the Zaporizhzhia region to Kyiv in 2022 described the situation as heartbreaking. She has continued paying utility bills on her apartment but says she will not return to register with occupying authorities. She is waiting for liberation.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Hundreds of thousands of displaced Ukrainians in occupied Ukraine at risk of losing homes under forced re-registration deadlines

events.title: Civil Unrest in Madrid
events.body:
Tens of thousands of people marched through Madrid on Sunday to protest soaring rents, high home prices, and a growing shortage of housing. Organizers said more than 100,000 people took part, while city authorities counted about 23,000. The march was organized by a tenants' union and backed by Spain's two largest trade unions.

Protesters say housing costs are rising far faster than the government is acting. According to EU statistics, housing costs rose nearly 13% in a single year. Spain's central bank estimates a shortage of around 700,000 homes, with more people being pushed into overcrowded or shared living conditions. Demonstrators also called for stricter limits on tourist rentals, which residents say are driving up prices in city centers.

The government recently approved a 7 billion euro plan to build more public housing and support young renters, but a separate measure to freeze rent increases failed in the legislature.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Tens of thousands of residents across Spain struggling with soaring housing costs and a severe shortage of affordable homes

events.title: Displacement in Kharkiv
events.body:
A doctor in Kharkiv went to bed on the night of February 23, 2022 after a normal day of work, picking up her daughter from school, cooking dinner, and preparing a lecture. The next morning she woke to explosions as Russia's full-scale invasion began.

She eventually fled Ukraine and made her way to the United Kingdom, where she worked to rebuild her life and career while helping other displaced Ukrainians navigate a new country and healthcare system.

Her story reflects the experience of millions of Ukrainians who left behind homes, professions, and communities overnight. Many who were doctors, teachers, or other professionals have had to start over, often in countries with different languages and licensing systems.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Millions of Ukrainian civilians displaced abroad rebuilding lives and careers after fleeing invasion

events.title: Armed Conflict in Nuseirat refugee camp
events.body:
An Israeli air strike on an apartment in the Nuseirat refugee camp in central Gaza killed a couple and their six-month-old child in the early hours of Sunday morning, according to medical workers. About ten more people were wounded in the attack. The bodies were received at Al-Aqsa Martyrs Hospital in Deir el-Balah.

Separately on Sunday, Israeli jets carried out air raids near a hospital in Deir el-Balah, and Israeli forces continued demolishing homes and civilian infrastructure in eastern Gaza. Palestinians were also fleeing their neighborhoods following forced displacement orders, with many running while carrying mattresses and personal belongings.

A ceasefire has technically been in effect since October, but Gaza health authorities say nearly 900 people have been killed since then. Talks aimed at a permanent end to the war remain stalled, and humanitarian organizations warn that Gaza's aid infrastructure continues to be at serious risk.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: for the families of those killed, including a young infant, and for civilians across Gaza facing daily attacks and displacement

events.title: Civil Unrest in Belgrade
events.body:
Large crowds gathered in central Belgrade on Saturday for an antigovernment rally, with clashes breaking out between protesters and riot police. The demonstrations were organised by a student-led movement whose protests began more than a year ago, sparked by a station canopy collapse in northern Serbia in November 2024 that killed 16 people. Many Serbs have blamed the tragedy on corruption during renovation work, and the movement has since grown into broader demands for accountability, rule of law, and early elections.

Authorities appeared to take steps to limit the turnout — the state railway company cancelled all trains to and from Belgrade on the day of the rally. Riot police in full gear were deployed around the city, including near a camp of government supporters set up outside the presidency building.

A Council of Europe human rights official, who visited Serbia the previous week, criticised the government's response to protests over the past year, including reports of police protecting masked attackers targeting journalists and demonstrators. The official said the overall human rights situation has worsened since a visit in April 2025.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Thousands of protesters and riot police clashing in Serbia amid demands for government accountability and rule of law

events.title: Armed Conflict in Quetta
events.body:
At least 24 people were killed and more than 50 injured when a car packed with explosives rammed a train in Quetta, the capital of Pakistan's Balochistan province. Soldiers were among the dead. The train was carrying military personnel and was passing a signal area when the blast occurred, causing two carriages to overturn and catch fire. Thick black smoke rose over the city as the fire spread.

The explosion badly damaged several nearby buildings and destroyed more than a dozen vehicles parked along the road. The attack happened in an area normally patrolled by security forces. A separatist group called the Balochistan Liberation Army claimed responsibility.

Balochistan has seen a long-running insurgency, and attacks on military targets are not uncommon in the province. Civilians in the area were also affected by the destruction left by the blast.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Dozens killed and wounded in Pakistan after a devastating car bomb struck a military train

events.title: Armed Conflict in Quetta
events.body:
A suicide car bombing struck a train carrying soldiers in Quetta, killing at least 24 people and wounding dozens more. Among the casualties were women and children who were also on board or near the train at the time of the attack.

A Baloch separatist group claimed responsibility for the bombing. The Balochistan region has seen a long-running insurgency, with separatist groups periodically targeting security forces and infrastructure.

The death toll and number of injured may rise as rescue and medical teams continue their response.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Soldiers, women, and children in Pakistan killed and wounded in a deadly train bombing

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Bahrain
events.body:
Bahrain has sentenced nine people to life in prison and two others to three years each after convicting them of working with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps. Authorities say some defendants photographed strategic sites on behalf of Iran, while others helped move money — including through cryptocurrency — into the country to fund operations. Prosecutors called the actions "hostile and terrorist acts."

The convictions come during a broader crackdown that began in March, following Iranian strikes on Bahrain after a wider conflict between Iran and the United States and Israel broke out in late February. Dozens more people have since been arrested, and over 60 had their citizenship revoked. A human rights group based in London called that step a violation of international law.

Bahrain is home to a large Shia Muslim population whose members have long said they face political and economic discrimination. The government denies this, and says Iran is working to stir up unrest inside the country.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Bahraini civilians amid mass arrests, citizenship revocations, and deepening sectarian tensions following a national security crackdown

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kyiv
events.body:
Russia launched one of its largest attacks on Kyiv since the war began, firing hundreds of drones and missiles across Ukraine overnight. The strikes caused damage across multiple sites, and Ukraine's president visited the affected areas in the aftermath.

Speaking to a news outlet at one of the damaged locations, Ukraine's president condemned the strikes, calling out the targeting of civilian infrastructure. Ukraine's government vowed to retaliate in response to the attack.

The scale of the assault marks a significant escalation, with hundreds of aerial weapons used in a single overnight campaign. Civilians across the country faced the impact of the strikes on homes and essential infrastructure.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians across Ukraine facing mass overnight drone and missile strikes on homes and essential infrastructure

events.title: Armed Conflict in Rigores, Bajo Aguan
events.body:
At least 19 people were killed at a palm plantation in Rigores, in Honduras's Bajo Aguan region, when gunmen opened fire on workers during the night. Residents reported hearing widespread gunfire, and a local rural leader described nine bodies scattered across the plantation in video footage, with investigators later confirming 13 victims in one area and six in another. Security officials said the workers were employed by an armed group that controlled the plantation.

The Bajo Aguan region has long been contested by rival groups fighting over palm oil profits and drug trafficking routes. Officials believe the massacre was linked to narcotrafficking rather than a land dispute, though local farmer groups claim corporations use armed groups to block residents from reclaiming disputed land.

In a separate incident near the Guatemalan border, four security officers and a civilian were killed in a shootout during an anti-gang operation. Honduras's parliament recently approved new laws allowing military involvement in public security and tougher measures against organized crime groups.
events.source_title: freemalaysiatoday.com
events.prayer: Palm plantation workers in Honduras massacred amid violent conflict over narcotrafficking and disputed land

events.title: Armed Conflict in Baisaran meadow, Pahalgam
events.body:
India's National Investigation Agency has filed a charge sheet detailing how three gunmen carried out an attack on tourists at a popular meadow near Pahalgam, killing 26 people. According to investigators, the attackers spent the night before the assault sheltering in a nearby hut, where a local resident's wife cooked them food. They questioned the family about a religious pilgrimage route, nearby security camps, and troop movements before leaving around 10 pm with food, blankets, and cooking supplies.

The following afternoon, the attackers sat under a tree and ate lunch near the park before positioning themselves and opening fire at 2:23 pm. Investigators say one attacker wore a camera on his head during the assault, and the group fired shots in celebration as they retreated.

The charge sheet names seven accused, including two local residents who were arrested and a Pakistan-based operative accused of sharing the exact location coordinates with the attackers. All three gunmen were killed in a security forces encounter in July 2025. The charge sheet was filed in December but its detailed contents have only recently become public.
events.source_title: moneycontrol.com
events.prayer: Families of 26 tourists killed in India during a brutal armed attack at a scenic meadow

events.title: South Korea
events.body:
South Korean police are seeking an arrest warrant for a popular YouTuber who allegedly used artificial intelligence to fabricate evidence against a well-known actor. Authorities say the YouTuber manipulated screenshots of text messages and created an AI-generated voice recording to falsely suggest the actor had been in a relationship with a young actress while she was still a minor. The actress died by suicide last year at the age of 24.

The fabricated content spread widely and effectively ended the actor's career. Police say his "social base and economic activities collapsed across the board." The actor has consistently denied the allegations and, at a press conference earlier this year, said he could not confess to something he did not do. He is reportedly still receiving psychiatric care.

Authorities allege the YouTuber spread the false claims for financial gain. The actor has since filed criminal complaints and lawsuits. A Disney+ series he had been filming when the scandal broke remains indefinitely postponed.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: South Korean actor's life and career devastated by AI-fabricated allegations spreading widely online

events.title: Civil Unrest in Ashdod
events.body:
More than 50 boats carrying pro-Palestinian activists set sail from Turkey last week as part of the Global Sumud Flotilla, aiming to deliver food and medical aid to Gaza by breaking Israel's maritime blockade. Israeli commandos boarded the vessels in international waters west of Cyprus on Monday and Tuesday. Over 422 people from 41 countries were brought to the port of Ashdod and held in detention before being deported on Thursday.

Since returning home, many activists have alleged they were beaten, humiliated, and sexually assaulted while in Israeli custody. Flotilla organizers claim at least 15 cases of sexual assault, including rape, along with broken bones and rubber bullet injuries at close range. Canada, Germany, Spain, and France have all raised concerns, with several governments confirming that some of their citizens received injuries.

Israel's prison service has dismissed the allegations as "false and entirely without factual basis," stating that all detainees were held lawfully with full regard for their basic rights. The BBC was unable to independently verify the allegations.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: for the detained activists, for justice and truth to emerge from the competing accounts, and for leaders in multiple countries to respond wisely

events.title: Civil Unrest in Ankara
events.body:
A court in Ankara opened trial proceedings against a journalist working for an international broadcaster, then released him from detention after roughly 92 days behind bars. The charges stem from social media posts accused of insulting the president, spreading misleading information, and disparaging state institutions — offenses that Turkish law treats as criminal and that the country's leadership has prosecuted thousands of times over the past two decades. The next hearing is scheduled for September 18, and the journalist's defense team is calling for an urgent acquittal.

The reporter had been held mostly alone in a high-security prison in Istanbul, far from his family in Ankara. He appeared at the hearing by video link rather than in person, which he described as a violation of his right to a fair defense.

Press freedom advocates welcomed the release but noted that other journalists remain imprisoned. Turkey ranks near the bottom of international press freedom indexes, and reporters focusing on human rights, the judicial system, and corruption face ongoing legal risks.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Journalists in Turkey facing criminal prosecution, imprisonment, and legal threats over their reporting

events.title: Civil Unrest in La Paz
events.body:
Weeks of highway blockades have escalated into serious unrest in La Paz, Bolivia's seat of government. Protesters have stormed public buildings, set off small dynamite charges, and erected dozens of blockades across the city. The disruptions have caused fuel and food shortages, left hospitals without oxygen cylinders, and prompted banks to close. Economists warn the crisis is deepening an economy already struggling with debt at 95% of GDP and a 14% drop in purchasing power over the past year.

The unrest stems from a mix of economic grievances. After nearly 20 years of socialist rule, voters chose center-right President Rodrigo Paz, who then cut fuel subsidies — nearly doubling prices — to stabilize the budget. Combined with inflation worsened by outside factors, this hit the poor hardest. A broad coalition of farmers, miners, teachers, and Indigenous groups united behind demands for wage increases and reinstated subsidies, eventually calling for the president's resignation.

Bolivia's largest trade union began organizing mass protests, and the government responded by seeking the arrest of the union's leader on terrorism charges. The US State Department has described the ongoing unrest as an "attempted coup." Residents in La Paz continue to face daily disruptions to basic services.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Civilians across Bolivia enduring fuel and food shortages, hospital crises, and escalating unrest amid economic hardship

events.title: Civil Unrest in Johannesburg
events.body:
Anti-foreigner tensions have resurfaced in South Africa, renewing debate over whether the government is doing enough to prevent violence against migrants. South Africa has seen repeated outbreaks targeting people from Zimbabwe, Nigeria, Somalia, Ethiopia, and other African countries. More than 60 people died in 2008 riots, and similar violence returned in 2015, 2016, and 2019, when armed mobs attacked foreign-owned businesses around Johannesburg, killing at least 12 people.

Government officials have condemned the violence and warned that civilians have no right to enforce immigration laws. But critics say official responses have been inconsistent, with limited arrests and rhetoric that sometimes adds to tensions rather than reducing them. Groups campaigning against undocumented migration have grown more influential, with some calling for mass deportations.

Researchers and advocates argue that migrants are being blamed for unemployment and poor services that existed long before they arrived. For many foreign nationals living in South Africa, fear has become part of daily life.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Foreign nationals across South Africa facing recurring mob violence, fear, and displacement amid anti-immigrant tensions

events.title: Displacement in Gaza
events.body:
A senior United Nations official warned the UN Security Council that Gaza is at risk of falling into a permanent state of limbo if plans for a political transition are not carried out. The warning came as the ceasefire in the territory remains fragile and humanitarian conditions continue to worsen.

The UN envoy told council members that delays in moving forward with the transition plan — which the Security Council has backed — will increase the suffering of people living in Gaza and make recovery harder to achieve.

No specific date was given for the events described, though the briefing to the Security Council took place on a Thursday. The situation leaves civilians in Gaza facing ongoing uncertainty about both their immediate needs and their longer-term future.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: for civilians in Gaza caught in ongoing instability, that aid can reach those in need and that a lasting path to recovery can be found

events.title: Armed Conflict in Starobilsk
events.body:
The United Nations expressed alarm over an overnight strike on a vocational school and dormitory in Starobilsk, a town in Ukraine's Luhansk region, which is currently under Russian occupation. Multiple civilians were killed or injured in the attack, including children.

The UN announced the strike on a Friday, though the exact date of the attack was not specified in available reports. Starobilsk is located in eastern Ukraine in territory that has been under occupation since the early stages of the broader conflict.

The targeting of a school dormitory where children were present drew particular concern from UN officials. Strikes on civilian infrastructure, including educational facilities, have been a recurring feature of the conflict in eastern Ukraine, and the presence of child casualties makes this incident especially serious.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Civilians and children in Ukraine killed or wounded in a strike on a school dormitory

events.title: Armed Conflict in Deir Qanoun al-Nahr
events.body:
An Israeli air strike killed two paramedics and a child in the southern Lebanese town of Deir Qanoun al-Nahr. Video of the incident was shared by Lebanese media.

According to Lebanese media reports, a second strike hit emergency crews who had arrived to treat casualties from an earlier attack. The paramedics were responding to the first strike when they were killed.

The deaths of medical responders alongside a child adds to growing concerns about civilian safety in southern Lebanon, where strikes have continued to affect residents and those who come to help them.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Paramedics and civilians in Lebanon killed amid ongoing strikes targeting emergency responders in the south

events.title: Civil Unrest in Ashdod
events.body:
Israeli naval forces intercepted roughly 430 volunteers aboard 50 ships in international waters on Tuesday, halting a flotilla carrying aid intended for Gaza. Those detained were brought to the Israeli port city of Ashdod, where a landing craft had been converted into a makeshift holding facility using shipping containers and barbed wire.

Flotilla organizers say they have documented at least 15 cases of sexual abuse among those held, including serious assaults on one of two prison vessels. Detainees also report being beaten, tased, denied water, stripped of clothing, and deprived of sleep over two days. Several were hospitalized with broken bones and injuries to their eyes and ears.

Germany confirmed some of its nationals were injured and called some allegations "serious." Italian prosecutors have opened an investigation into possible kidnapping and sexual assault. A United Nations spokesperson said those still detained should be released and that those responsible for mistreatment should be held to account. Hundreds of activists were deported from Israel on Thursday.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: for the detained activists who report abuse, for accountability where wrongdoing occurred, and for the safe return of all those still held

events.title: Displacement in Beirut
events.body:
A man who is an amputee has been displaced multiple times by the ongoing conflict between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon. He is now living in a makeshift camp in central Beirut, having lost income and sustained damage to his home.

His situation reflects a broader crisis across the country. More than 1.6 million people have been displaced in Lebanon amid continued Israeli attacks.

For someone with a physical disability, finding shelter and basic support is especially difficult. With no clear end to the conflict in sight, many displaced people face deepening uncertainty about whether they can ever return home.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Over 1.6 million displaced people in Lebanon, including those with disabilities, facing homelessness and uncertainty

events.title: Civil Unrest in Havana
events.body:
Thousands of people gathered outside the United States embassy in Havana to rally in support of former Cuban president Raul Castro. The demonstration came after the United States filed criminal charges against him over his alleged role in the 1996 shooting down of two civilian aircraft operated by a Cuban-American exile organization.

The crowd gathered to show opposition to the indictment, which relates to an incident nearly three decades ago in which two planes were downed. The charges represent a significant escalation in tensions between the two countries.

The protest drew a large public turnout in the Cuban capital, signaling strong local sentiment around the case. Residents in Havana were present in the area around the embassy during the demonstration.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Thousands of Cubans amid heightened US-Cuba tensions following decades-old criminal indictment

events.title: Civil Unrest in La Paz
events.body:
Bolivia is in the grip of a deepening political crisis just six months after a new president took office. What began as separate protests over wages, contaminated fuel, and land laws has grown into a coordinated movement demanding the president's resignation. Since early May, hundreds of protesters have surrounded the capital, La Paz, with road blockades — running around 20 simultaneous pickets daily. Major labor unions have joined Indigenous groups from the highlands in the effort to pressure the president to step down.

Residents of La Paz and the neighboring city of El Alto are facing food shortages, school closures, and disrupted transportation. The government has so far ruled out declaring a state of emergency, even as some residents have called for stronger action to restore order.

Analysts point to a broader sense of exclusion felt by Indigenous and working-class communities, who say the current administration has governed in favor of business elites rather than the groups that helped bring it to power.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Bolivian civilians facing food shortages, school closures, and blocked transportation amid ongoing political unrest

events.title: Armed Conflict in Kyiv
events.body:
A recent Russian aerial assault on Ukraine — described as the largest sustained attack of the war — involved 1,500 drones and 56 missiles fired within 48 hours. Twenty-four civilians were killed, including two sisters, ages 12 and 17, whose apartment block was destroyed. Their father had already died fighting at the front, leaving their mother as the family's only survivor. A funeral for the girls was held in Kyiv this week as air raid sirens sounded in the background.

Ukraine says its air defenses intercepted 94% of the long-range drones and 73% of the missiles in that attack. The country has built up a layered defense system combining Western-supplied weapons, an AI-powered tracking platform called Sky Map, and cheap domestically produced interceptor drones. Ukraine now makes more than 1,000 of these interceptor drones per day, each costing around $1,000.

Private companies are joining the effort as well, building networks of sensor-equipped towers and remotely controlled weapons, particularly near the Russian border. Russian attacks on Ukraine's power grid last winter left millions without electricity.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians across Ukraine grieving and displaced amid the largest aerial assault of the ongoing war

events.title: Ituri province
events.body:
