An Ebola outbreak in eastern Congo has killed between 139 and 159 people out of roughly 600 suspected cases, according to the World Health Organization and Congolese health officials. The outbreak is centered in Ituri province and is caused by a rare strain called Bundibugyo, for which no vaccine currently exists. The WHO has declared it a public health emergency of international concern, though not a pandemic.

The outbreak has prompted the United States, which is co-hosting this summer's World Cup, to ban entry to non-Americans who have recently been in Congo, Uganda, or South Sudan. In response, Congo's national football team cancelled a pre-tournament training camp in the capital, Kinshasa — roughly 1,800 kilometers from the epicenter — and relocated preparations to Europe. No Ebola cases have been reported in Kinshasa.

The WHO says a vaccine for this Ebola strain could take up to nine months to develop, leaving affected communities in the east without that protection for the foreseeable future.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Hundreds of Ebola patients in Congo facing a deadly outbreak with no available vaccine

events.title: Displacement in Port-au-Prince
events.body:
Around 30,000 people have been forced to flee their homes in and around Port-au-Prince, Haiti's capital, following a new wave of attacks by armed gangs over the past ten days. The violence has driven families from their neighborhoods and added thousands more to the already large number of displaced people in the country.

The displacement comes as Haiti is also facing a deepening hunger crisis, making conditions especially hard for those who have lost access to their homes, belongings, and livelihoods. People who have fled are now among the most vulnerable, needing food, shelter, and basic safety. The combination of gang violence and food insecurity continues to put enormous pressure on civilians across the Port-au-Prince area.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Tens of thousands of displaced Haitians fleeing gang violence amid a deepening hunger crisis

events.title: Armed Conflict in Dnipro
events.body:
Russian missile and drone strikes hit the city of Dnipro overnight, killing people and destroying aid supplies, according to the UN refugee agency (UNHCR). The agency's representative in Ukraine strongly condemned the attacks.

The strikes drew an immediate response from international humanitarian organizations working in the country. The loss of aid materials is a significant blow to relief efforts in a city that has faced repeated attacks during the war.

No further details about the number of casualties or the specific aid supplies destroyed were provided in the available summary. Civilians in Dnipro and surrounding areas continue to face danger from ongoing aerial attacks.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Civilians in Ukraine enduring deadly overnight missile and drone strikes destroying vital humanitarian aid supplies

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Tehran
events.body:
Iran's Foreign Ministry said it is reviewing a response from the United States passed through Pakistan as part of ongoing efforts to end the war. Pakistan's army chief was expected to travel to Tehran the same day to continue mediation. Iran's president said all paths to a diplomatic solution remain open, while warning that pressure and threats will not force the country into surrender.

At the same time, Iran's newly created Persian Gulf Strait Authority announced a "supervision area" in the Strait of Hormuz, requiring ships to obtain permission before passing through the vital waterway. The move adds another layer of tension to an already volatile situation.

Separately, anger is spreading internationally after Israel's far-right national security minister shared a video appearing to mock activists from a Gaza-bound aid flotilla while they were allegedly being mistreated in Israeli custody. Rights groups condemned the post as inflammatory.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: for leaders on all sides to find a path toward peace and for civilians living under the threat of continued conflict

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Israel
events.body:
Israeli officials and media are openly discussing the possibility of renewing military conflict with Iran, even as the United States pushes to hold a ceasefire in place. A television anchor on a right-wing Israeli channel publicly revealed what appeared to be confidential plans for a strike on Iran, including a claimed uranium storage site. Members of the Israeli parliament criticized the disclosure, and the anchor later said his remarks were hypothetical.

Israeli leadership has held at least two security cabinet meetings to discuss restarting hostilities. Reports say a phone call between Israel's prime minister and the US president left the Israeli leader deeply frustrated after Washington signaled it wants a truce regardless of Israeli objections.

A recent poll found most Israelis believe ending the war too soon harms their country's security, and many expect fighting to resume. Analysts note that Israeli public opinion has long been shaped by a view of Iran as an existential danger, making political pressure for renewed conflict significant even if military action depends heavily on US support.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Israeli and Iranian tensions escalating as ceasefire pressure mounts and renewed conflict looms

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Havana
events.body:
The United States has significantly escalated pressure on Cuba in recent months. A federal indictment was filed against former Cuban president Raúl Castro, charging him with conspiracy to kill US nationals, four counts of murder, and two counts of aircraft destruction related to a 1996 incident in which Cuban jets allegedly shot down planes flown by Cuban exiles, killing four American men. The move comes alongside increased US surveillance flights near Cuba, a US Senate vote that narrowly failed to limit presidential authority to use military force against the island, and executive orders declaring Cuba a national security threat.

In response, Cuba's Civil Defence released a guide called *The Family Guide for Protection Against Military Aggression*, outlining safety steps for families in the event of a US attack. Cuban leaders have warned that any military action would result in a "bloodbath."

Analysts note that Cuba faces serious economic hardships, including fuel shortages and rolling blackouts, but that its military is considered better trained than Venezuela's. Cuba's national defence doctrine calls for the entire civilian population to take part in resistance, raising concerns about civilian casualties if conflict breaks out.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Cuban civilians facing heightened risk of conflict and economic hardship amid escalating military tensions with the United States

events.title: Ituri
events.body:
An Ebola outbreak centered in eastern Congo has grown to 600 suspected cases and 139 suspected deaths, according to the World Health Organization. The confirmed cases are concentrated in Congo's Ituri and North Kivu provinces, with two additional confirmed cases in Uganda's capital, where both patients had traveled from Congo.

The outbreak involves the Bundibugyo species of Ebola, which has not been seen in over a decade and has no approved vaccine. Two experimental vaccines are in development, but neither has gone through clinical trials, and health officials say a vaccine could be nine months away. A vaccine for a different Ebola species may offer some protection.

Health facilities in the affected areas are reporting they are full of suspected cases and lack space to treat patients. Healthcare workers are among those who have died, and staff say they are still operating without adequate protective equipment, even as supplies have begun to arrive.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Overwhelmed health facilities in Congo facing a growing Ebola outbreak with no approved vaccine available

events.title: Armed Conflict in Lebanon
events.body:
In Lebanon, rescue workers and paramedics have developed a somber routine before heading out to respond to air strikes: saying goodbye to one another. Videos of these farewells have been widely shared, drawing attention to the growing danger faced by those trying to help the wounded.

The risks for aid workers have risen sharply since hostilities between Hezbollah and Israel intensified, with fighting reported to have resumed around early March. First responders now suit up in flak jackets before heading to strike sites, knowing they may not return.

The situation highlights how civilian emergency workers are operating under conditions of extreme danger. For communities across Lebanon, the people responsible for pulling survivors from rubble and rushing the injured to hospitals are themselves targets of the same violence they are trying to address.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Lebanese rescue workers and paramedics risking their lives amid ongoing airstrikes to reach the wounded

events.title: Armed Conflict in Beirut
events.body:
An Israeli airstrike hit a residential building in Beirut, killing five people. Two families who lost relatives in the attack are now seeking justice for what happened to their loved ones.

The strike was part of what the source describes as one of the largest waves of Israeli attacks across Lebanon. It took place hours after a ceasefire between the United States and Iran was announced.

The two families affected grew up as neighbors and lost relatives in the same strike. The deaths in a residential area highlight the ongoing danger faced by civilians in Beirut even amid broader diplomatic developments in the region.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Two families in Lebanon grieving loved ones killed in a residential airstrike amid ongoing conflict

events.title: Armed Conflict in southern Lebanon
events.body:
At least eight people were killed in Israeli airstrikes across southern Lebanon on Wednesday. Fighter jets struck the village of Doueir, killing five people and destroying several homes. Two more people were killed near a hospital in the village of Tibnin, and one person on a motorcycle was killed in a drone strike in the village of Burj Shemali in the Tyre district. The Red Cross recovered one more body near the town of Shebaa.

These strikes came just hours after at least 16 people, including three women and three children, were killed in similar attacks on Tuesday. Lebanese authorities say that since March 2, Israeli operations have killed more than 3,000 people, injured over 9,000, and displaced more than 1.6 million — roughly one in five people in the country.

The attacks are continuing despite a U.S.-mediated ceasefire that was recently extended to early July. Israeli forces have also been expanding operations into the western Bekaa Valley, where entire villages have been destroyed.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians across Lebanon facing ongoing airstrikes, mass displacement, and a deepening humanitarian crisis

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Tehran
events.body:
Iran's stock market reopened this week after nearly three months of closure, following strikes by the United States and Israel that damaged key industries. The reopening was partial — about 36 percent of the market's main companies, including major petrochemical, steel, and utility firms, were kept offline to shield investors from the full impact of war-related damage. Trading limits were set at three percent gain or loss per day to prevent dramatic swings.

The two-day reopening showed modest gains, and more buyers than sellers showed up. However, economists cautioned that the positive signs may reflect how badly the economy was already struggling rather than a true recovery. Steep inflation, a weakened national currency, and war damage to infrastructure have all taken a toll. Smaller brokerage firms are in particular difficulty, and many traders were left in limbo after their contracts expired during the closure.

Authorities acknowledged that another wave of attacks could force the market to shut again.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Iranian citizens navigating severe economic hardship amid war damage, inflation, and a fragile partial market reopening

events.title: Civil Unrest in Bolivia — La Paz
events.body:
Thousands of people gathered in Bolivia's capital demanding the resignation of President Rodrigo Paz. Farmers, miners, teachers, and members of indigenous communities were among those who took to the streets, calling for higher wages and an end to the privatization of state-owned companies. Police responded with tear gas to control the crowds.

Video from La Paz showed protesters chanting, lighting fires, and throwing objects. The demonstrations are part of weeks of ongoing unrest driven by economic pressures across the country.

The country's attorney general announced that an arrest warrant would be sought for the leader of Bolivia's largest labor union, accusing him of terrorism and inciting protests against the government. The move signals an escalation in the government's response to the growing protest movement.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Thousands of civilians across Bolivia protesting amid economic hardship, tear gas, and escalating government crackdowns

events.title: Civil Unrest in Pakistan 06/01/2024 — Islamabad
events.body:
A court in Islamabad has sentenced a 23-year-old man to death for the murder of a 17-year-old social media creator who had rejected his advances. The man broke into her home and shot her dead after she refused to meet with him, despite him traveling to the capital days before the killing. The court also ordered him to pay roughly $9,000 in compensation to her family. Her father called the verdict "a lesson for all such criminals in society."

The victim had over a million followers on TikTok and was known for lighthearted content about fashion and everyday life. While her death drew widespread outrage, some online voices blamed her for her work as a creator, with a portion of mostly male users citing religious reasons and calling for her accounts to be removed.

Human rights activists say the murder reflects a broader pattern of violence against women in Pakistan, and warn that social media has become an increasingly threatening space for female content creators in the country.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Women and female content creators in Pakistan facing gender-based violence and online harassment

events.title: Congo — Ituri
events.body:
The World Health Organization says there are now 600 suspected Ebola cases and 139 suspected deaths across the Democratic Republic of Congo and neighboring Uganda. Fifty-one cases have been confirmed in DRC's eastern Ituri and North Kivu provinces, and two confirmed cases have been found in Uganda's capital, Kampala — both in people who had traveled from DRC, one of whom has died. The outbreak is believed to have started a couple of months ago.

The strain involved is the Bundibugyo species of Ebola, which has not been seen in over a decade. There is no approved vaccine or targeted treatment for this strain, making it harder to manage than outbreaks DRC has faced before. In past Bundibugyo outbreaks, roughly one in three infected people died.

Health workers in the region say some facilities are already overwhelmed, and staff are treating patients without adequate protective equipment. Residents in the affected areas say they have already changed daily habits — such as avoiding handshakes — as awareness of the danger grows. Officials expect case numbers to continue rising.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Thousands of people in Congo and Uganda facing a rapidly spreading Ebola outbreak with no approved vaccine or treatment

events.title: Nigeria — Kaduna
events.body:
A former Nigerian government minister who had been hiding from authorities was arrested in the northern city of Kaduna, days after a court sentenced him in his absence to 75 years in prison for laundering the equivalent of about $24.6 million. Prosecutors say the money came from government-financed hydroelectric projects. He is now in custody, and the court also ordered him to repay roughly $16 million to the state.

The arrest is part of a wider pattern of anti-corruption cases in Nigeria, where the national anti-fraud agency has pursued dozens of former officials. However, research covering 393 corruption cases over 14 years shows that more than 60% remain unresolved. Appointed officials are far more likely to be convicted than elected ones — of 33 former governors prosecuted, only six have been convicted, and some of those convictions were later overturned or pardoned.

Legal experts say wealthy, well-connected defendants often use delays and political influence to avoid final judgments, sometimes stretching cases out for a decade or more.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Nigerian citizens and anti-corruption efforts amid systemic delays leaving over 60% of fraud cases unresolved

events.title: Civil Unrest in Kenya 05/19/2025 — Kenya
events.body:
A nationwide transport strike erupted in Kenya after fuel prices rose sharply — petrol up 20 percent and diesel nearly 40 percent — linked to disruptions in global oil supply routes. Bus operators, particularly "matatu" minibus drivers who carry most of Kenya's commuters, launched the strike on Monday. Protests broke out across the country the same day.

Four people were killed and more than 30 were injured during Monday's demonstrations. By Tuesday, police had arrested more than 700 people in connection with the unrest. Rights groups, including Amnesty International, called on security forces to show restraint.

The strike was suspended for one week to allow talks between the government and transport stakeholders. The unrest also disrupted Kenya's main trade corridor, with truck drivers refusing to move cargo out of fear their vehicles would be attacked. About a third of Kenya's roughly 50 million people live in poverty, making steep fuel price increases especially difficult for ordinary households.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Kenyan civilians and commuters facing deadly unrest, mass arrests, and crippling transport disruption amid steep fuel price rises

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Philippines 05/20/2026 — Manila
events.body:
The Philippine Supreme Court has cleared the way for the potential arrest of a senator wanted by the International Criminal Court for crimes against humanity. The senator, a former national police chief, was the lead enforcer of a drug crackdown during the 2016–2022 presidency of Rodrigo Duterte, in which the ICC estimates between 12,000 and 30,000 people were killed. The senator has denied involvement in illegal killings.

The ruling came after a dramatic sequence of events: the senator had been in hiding for six months, then took refuge inside the Senate building for several days before fleeing after a shooting incident between government agents and Senate security personnel. The Supreme Court refused to block the ICC arrest warrant, though it noted some legal questions in the senator's petition remain unresolved.

The senator's lawyers say they will pursue further legal options. The government has said it will seek to enforce the warrant. Duterte himself has been held at the ICC in The Hague since March 2025.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Families in the Philippines seeking justice and accountability after thousands killed in the drug war crackdown

events.title: Civil Unrest in South Korea — Suwon
events.body:
Nearly 48,000 workers at Samsung Electronics in South Korea are preparing to strike for 18 days over a dispute about bonus payments. The workers' union wants the company to remove a cap on bonuses and set aside a share of annual profits for workers. Samsung has called the demands excessive, and government-mediated talks ended without an agreement.

The strike is drawing national attention because Samsung is a massive part of South Korea's economy, generating revenues equal to about 12.5 percent of the country's total economic output. The country's central bank warned the strike could reduce national economic growth by half a percentage point and cause around $20 billion in losses, with chip production taking weeks to recover if fully halted.

A court partially limited the strike by requiring minimum staffing at some essential production units. The union said it would continue pushing for a deal even while striking, and the prime minister warned the economic damage could be severe if the walkout proceeds as planned.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Tens of thousands of workers in South Korea facing prolonged strike and economic uncertainty amid unresolved labor dispute

events.title: Congo — Ituri and North Kivu
events.body:
An Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda has grown to at least 600 suspected cases and 139 suspected deaths, according to the World Health Organization. The WHO has declared the situation a public health emergency of international concern. Health officials believe the outbreak began several months ago, with the first suspected death reported on April 20. A possible super-spreader event at a funeral or healthcare facility is thought to have made things worse.

The outbreak is caused by the Bundibugyo strain of Ebola, for which no vaccine or approved treatment currently exists. Confirmed cases in the DRC are concentrated in the northern provinces of Ituri and North Kivu. Uganda has confirmed two cases in its capital, Kampala, both involving people who had traveled from the DRC; one of those cases was fatal. A medical missionary who contracted Ebola in the DRC is being flown to Germany for treatment.

The WHO says its immediate focus is tracing all chains of transmission to understand the full scale of the outbreak and get care to those affected.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Hundreds of people in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda affected by a deadly Ebola outbreak with no available vaccine or treatment

events.title: Armed Conflict in Israel 05/16/2026 — southern Lebanon
events.body:
On May 16, the Israeli military reported that Hezbollah had launched what it called hostile aircraft, mortar shells, and explosive drones toward Israeli soldiers along the border area.

In response, Israeli forces carried out strikes on approximately 100 Hezbollah targets across several areas in southern Lebanon within a single day.

The exchange signals continued active fighting along the Israel-Lebanon border, raising concern for civilians living in the affected areas of southern Lebanon.
events.source_title: International Crisis Group
events.prayer: Civilians in Lebanon caught in intensifying cross-border strikes and ongoing military exchanges

events.title: Civil Unrest in Iran — Iraqi-Iranian border region
events.body:
During the early stages of a US-Israeli military campaign against Iran that began in March, US President Donald Trump claimed that weapons meant for Iranian protesters were sent through Kurdish intermediaries but were kept by Kurdish forces. Kurdish leaders and organizations across Iraq, Turkey, Syria, and Iran have all flatly denied receiving any weapons from the United States.

Kurdish party officials described the claims as logistically impossible, pointing to heavily militarized borders, strong Iranian security presence, and security agreements between Tehran and Baghdad that would make cross-border arms transfers extremely difficult. Several leaders called the accusations "psychological warfare."

Analysts warn that referring to all Kurds as a single unified group misrepresents a population of over 30 million people spread across multiple countries, each with distinct political parties and dynamics. Researchers caution that collective blame could inflame public opinion against Kurdish communities and put them at greater physical risk.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Kurdish communities across Iraq, Iran, Syria, and Turkey facing heightened risk from collective blame amid disputed weapons claims

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Cuba — Cuba
events.body:
Cuba's government has released an official emergency guide telling families how to prepare for a possible military attack by the United States. The guide recommends packing a bag with documents, food for three days, water, a flashlight, matches, and medications, and suggests identifying nearby shelters in case of airstrikes. Many Cubans reacted with frustration and disbelief on social media, pointing out that basic survival is already a daily struggle amid widespread power outages, food shortages, and limited healthcare.

The release of the guide comes amid rising tension between Havana and Washington. U.S. officials have made pointed statements about Cuba, and reports emerged that Cuba had acquired over 300 military drones being evaluated for use near the U.S. base at Guantanamo. Analysts quoted in the source say the drone numbers are not militarily significant given the gap in capabilities between the two countries.

Experts believe the greater risk may be a humanitarian one. If unrest were to trigger mass migration, the political consequences for the United States could be severe. Analysts say direct military confrontation remains unlikely for now.
events.source_title: World | Deutsche Welle
events.prayer: Cuban families already struggling with shortages amid rising military tensions and government emergency warnings

events.title: Armed Conflict in Ukraine — Ukraine
events.body:
A senior United Nations official warned the Security Council on Tuesday that the war in Ukraine, now in its fifth year, "is becoming deadlier by the day."

The briefing was delivered to the UN's most powerful body, which holds responsibility for international peace and security. No further details about specific incidents or casualty figures were included in the summary available.

The warning signals that conditions on the ground continue to worsen for people living in affected areas of Ukraine, with no indication of a near-term change in the situation.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Civilians across Ukraine enduring escalating conflict now entering its fifth year with worsening conditions

events.title: Armed Conflict in United States — San Diego
events.body:
A security guard was shot and killed while trying to prevent armed attackers from entering the Islamic Centre of San Diego. Authorities credited his actions with saving the lives of others inside the mosque.

The guard, a father of eight, has been widely remembered as a hero. Tributes have poured in following his death, honoring his courage in the face of the attack.

No further details about the gunmen or the number of other casualties were included in the source. The shooting has left the local community in mourning.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: A security guard killed in a shooting attack at a mosque in the United States, leaving a community in mourning

events.title: Civil Unrest in Israel 05/19/2026 — West Bank
events.body:
Israel's Finance Minister announced plans to forcibly displace Palestinians from a village in the occupied West Bank after saying he had received an International Criminal Court arrest warrant for war crimes. The minister called the ICC action a "declaration of war."

The announcement came in direct response to the warrant request and signals the possibility of further displacement of Palestinian civilians in the West Bank. No timeline for the proposed action was given in the report.

Residents of the unnamed village face an uncertain situation as international legal proceedings and Israeli government responses unfold. Palestinians living in that area remain at risk of being removed from their homes.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Palestinians in the West Bank facing threatened forced displacement amid escalating international legal tensions

events.title: Armed Conflict in United States 05/18/2026 — San Diego
events.body:
Two gunmen attacked the Islamic Center of San Diego on Monday, killing three men who worked to stop them and protect others. Authorities confirmed the identities of all three victims: a longtime mosque employee who called police before he was killed, a neighbor who rushed over when he heard shots, and a security guard who engaged the attackers directly. The two alleged attackers, ages 17 and 18, were later found dead of apparent self-inflicted gunshot wounds. Police are investigating the attack as a hate crime.

Officials said the actions of the three men likely saved the lives of 140 children inside a school on the property. By drawing the gunmen toward the parking area, two of the victims helped slow the attack and divert them away from others.

More than 30 guns and a crossbow were recovered from locations connected to the attackers. The mosque complex, the largest in the county, remained closed Tuesday as the investigation continued. Mosque leaders said the community had taken precautions but did not anticipate an attack of this scale.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Muslim community in the United States mourning three men killed protecting others during a hate-motivated attack on a mosque

events.title: Armed Conflict in Ukraine — Moscow region
events.body:
Ukrainian forces carried out a large strike on the Moscow region, penetrating Russian air defenses in one of the most significant attacks deep inside Russian territory since the war began. The strike came just one week after concerns about a potential Ukrainian attack led Russia to scale back its annual Victory Day parade.

Ukraine has also been targeting Russian oil facilities and military supply lines in a sustained effort to cut off resources reaching the front lines. At the same time, Russia has continued launching missiles and drones at sites across Ukraine.

The war is now in its fifth year, with both sides showing no sign of stepping back. Civilians on both sides of the border remain at risk from ongoing strikes.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Ukraine and Russia endangered by escalating long-range strikes as war enters its fifth year

events.title: Armed Conflict in Ukraine 05/19/2026 — Pryluky, Hlukhiv, Buryn, Borisovka
events.body:
The United Nations told the Security Council this week that 15,850 people have been killed in Ukraine since Russia's full-scale invasion began in February 2022 — including 791 children. A UN official noted the real toll is likely much higher. Peace talks remain stalled, and a recent short ceasefire quickly broke down.

On the same day as the UN briefing, at least six people were killed across Ukraine in Russian attacks. A teenager was among three people killed in a ballistic missile strike on Pryluky in the Chernihiv region. Two more were killed in a drone attack on Hlukhiv in Sumy region, and one person was killed when a drone struck a civilian car in Buryn.

In Russia, two people were killed and six injured in a Ukrainian drone attack on a village in Belgorod region. Ukraine also struck an industrial facility in Yaroslavl region, and a gas station in Bryansk region left two people injured.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians across Ukraine facing ongoing missile and drone strikes as peace talks remain stalled and casualties mount

events.title: Armed Conflict in Iran — Lavan Island
events.body:
An attack on Iran's Lavan oil refinery last month caused a significant oil spill that has now reached the shores of nearby Shidvar Island, a protected nature reserve. Newly released video footage shows oil washed up along the coastline, with wildlife visibly trapped and suffocating in the contamination.

The full extent of the environmental damage is still becoming clear, but the images show serious harm to a sensitive natural area. The spill adds a prolonged environmental consequence to the original attack on the refinery, affecting both the local ecosystem and communities in the surrounding area.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Communities and wildlife in Iran affected by a major oil spill spreading across a protected coastal nature reserve

events.title: Civil Unrest in Bolivia 05/19/2026 — La Paz
events.body:
Thousands of people took to the streets of La Paz, Bolivia's administrative capital, demanding the president's resignation as the country faces its worst economic crisis in 40 years. Farmers, miners, teachers, public sector workers, and Indigenous communities joined the protests on Monday, with road blockades cutting off supplies of food, fuel, and medicine to the city. Year-on-year inflation has reached 14 percent, squeezing household budgets and deepening public anger.

Riot police used tear gas against demonstrators who were trying to reach the main government square, while some protesters threw stones and small explosives in response. At least two people were reported injured, and more than 100 people were detained nationwide. Protesters were also seen removing equipment from a government office.

The unrest follows the president's move to cut longstanding fuel subsidies, a step officials say was necessary to protect the country's foreign currency reserves. The decision has so far worsened fuel shortages rather than eased them, adding to the pressure on ordinary Bolivians already struggling with rising costs.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Bolivians across the country amid economic crisis, fuel shortages, rising prices, and widespread civil unrest

events.title: Congo — DRC and Uganda
events.body:
An American medical missionary who contracted Ebola while working in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is being flown to Germany for treatment at a Berlin hospital. The patient was identified as being associated with the Serge Christian mission organization. Six others considered high-risk contacts are also being sent to Europe — most to Germany, with one going to the Czech Republic — where they will be quarantined during a monitoring period.

The current outbreak involves a rare strain of Ebola affecting both the DRC and Uganda. Health officials report more than 130 deaths and over 500 suspected cases. The World Health Organization's director-general described the situation as deeply concerning, and the WHO's lead official in the DRC expects the outbreak to continue for at least two more months.

The DRC is awaiting shipments of an experimental vaccine from the United States and the United Kingdom. Authorities say the risk to people in the US remains low at this time.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Ebola outbreak across DRC and Uganda with over 130 deaths and 500 suspected cases still spreading

events.title: Coup/Crisis in Egypt — Egypt
events.body:
Egypt is caught in a difficult position as a U.S.-Israeli war with Iran reshapes the Middle East. The Egyptian government has tried to act as a mediator alongside Turkey, Pakistan, and Saudi Arabia, helping relay messages between Washington and Tehran while stopping short of taking sides. Officials have visited Gulf capitals, condemned Iranian missile and drone attacks on Gulf states, and even sent fighter jets to the region as a show of support.

But Gulf Arab partners — particularly the UAE — say Egypt's backing has not gone far enough. That matters enormously because Gulf investment is keeping Egypt's economy afloat, including a $35 billion Emirati real estate deal and billions more from Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and Qatar.

Ordinary Egyptians are already feeling the pain. Suez Canal revenues dropped 38 percent in early 2026, the Egyptian pound has lost significant value, fuel prices have risen, and electricity cuts have darkened city streets. If Gulf frustration hardens into frozen financial support, the economic strain on everyday people could deepen considerably.
events.source_title: International Crisis Group
events.prayer: Egyptian civilians facing deepening economic hardship as regional conflict strains trade, currency, and energy access

events.title: Armed Conflict in Lebanon 05/16/2026 — southern Lebanon
events.body:
On May 16, the Israeli military reported that Hezbollah had launched aircraft, mortar shells, and explosive drones near Israeli soldiers in southern Lebanon over the course of the previous day.

In response, Israeli forces said they struck around 100 Hezbollah targets across several areas in southern Lebanon. No casualty figures were included in the report.

The exchange signals continued active fighting in southern Lebanon, where residents face danger from both incoming fire and airstrikes.
events.source_title: International Crisis Group
events.prayer: Residents of southern Lebanon caught in active cross-border fighting involving airstrikes and explosive drone attacks

events.title: Civil Unrest in Kenya — Nairobi
events.body:
Four people were killed and at least 30 injured during nationwide transport strikes in Kenya over sharply rising fuel prices. The interior minister said 348 people were arrested as protesters blocked roads with burning tyres and barricades across the country. Police used tear gas to disperse crowds in parts of Nairobi and elsewhere, and six officers were reported injured.

Thousands of commuters were left stranded at bus stops, with major roads in Nairobi largely empty. Businesses stayed shut and schools asked students to remain home. A bus carrying passengers from Tanzania was unable to reach the capital and was left waiting in Kajiado, about 75 kilometres outside the city.

The strikes were called by a transport operators' alliance after authorities raised fuel prices by more than 20% to record highs. The alliance is demanding the increases be reversed and prices cut by around 35%. Residents described fares doubling and the cost of daily life becoming increasingly difficult to manage.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Kenyan civilians caught amid deadly transport strikes and protests over record fuel price hikes

events.title: Armed Conflict in Lebanon — southern Lebanon and the Bekaa Valley
events.body:
Lebanon's health ministry announced that more than 3,000 people have been killed in the country since fighting between Israel and Hezbollah escalated in early March. The toll reached 3,020, with over 400 of those deaths occurring after a ceasefire took effect on April 17.

Despite an agreement reached last Friday to extend the truce by 45 days, strikes continued across southern Lebanon and the Bekaa Valley over the weekend, hitting more than two dozen villages — only nine of which received evacuation warnings beforehand. Hezbollah also launched drone attacks targeting a barracks in northern Israel, and claimed several operations against Israeli troops in the south. Israel reported one soldier killed, bringing its military losses since March to 20, along with four civilians.

Israeli ground forces remain in a strip of Lebanese territory roughly 10 kilometers deep along the border. The two sides are expected to resume negotiations in early June.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Thousands of civilians in Lebanon killed amid ongoing strikes despite a fragile and repeatedly violated ceasefire

events.title: Armed Conflict in United States — Gulf region
events.body:
A US military attack on Iran planned for Tuesday was called off after leaders of Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates asked the US president to hold back while negotiations continue. The president said he had been told a deal "very acceptable" to the US would be reached, but warned a "full, large scale assault" could resume at any moment if talks fail. A senior Iranian military commander responded by warning the US against making "strategic mistakes."

The conflict began when US and Israeli forces launched major airstrikes on Iran on February 28. Iran retaliated with drones and missiles targeting Israel and US positions across the Gulf. Iran has since closed the Strait of Hormuz — a waterway carrying roughly 20% of the world's oil and gas — pushing fuel prices higher worldwide.

Gulf Arab states remain deeply concerned that further US strikes could trigger Iranian retaliation against airports, energy facilities, and desalination plants that provide drinking water to millions. Talks are continuing through Pakistani mediators, and a ceasefire reached in April has mostly held despite occasional exchanges of fire.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Millions across the Gulf region caught between ongoing US-Iran conflict and threats to energy and water supplies

events.title: Congo — eastern Congo
events.body:
A new Ebola outbreak in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo has been declared an international health emergency by the World Health Organization. The outbreak is unfolding in a region already struggling with widespread hunger and displacement, compounding the challenge of containing the disease.

Global disease experts warned that the risk of another worldwide pandemic — similar to the coronavirus crisis that began in 2019 — is growing over time. The combination of ongoing conflict, food insecurity, and population movement in eastern Congo creates conditions that make it harder to track and stop the spread of infectious disease.

Communities in the region are already under severe strain, and the arrival of an Ebola outbreak adds another layer of danger for civilians, aid workers, and medical teams trying to respond.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Civilians in the Democratic Republic of Congo facing an Ebola outbreak amid conflict, hunger, and displacement

events.title: Displacement in Mexico — Latin America and Caribbean
events.body:
A new report from the World Meteorological Organization warns that record-breaking heat, deadly floods, severe drought, and stronger hurricanes are putting millions of people across Latin America and the Caribbean at serious risk. The threats include growing food insecurity, water shortages, and forced displacement.

The report describes conditions worsening across the region, with extreme weather events becoming more intense and more frequent. Communities already facing poverty and limited resources are among the hardest hit.

Hunger and lack of clean water are among the most immediate concerns flagged by the report, alongside the danger of people being driven from their homes by floods or drought. The combined pressure of these conditions is stretching the ability of families and communities to cope.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Millions across Latin America and the Caribbean displaced or at risk from extreme weather, hunger, and water shortages

events.title: Displacement in Palestine — West Bank
events.body:
A ceasefire between Israel and Hamas has reduced but not stopped the violence in Gaza, according to a senior UN human rights official speaking in Geneva. Killings and destruction are still being reported in the territory.

In the occupied West Bank, the situation has become especially alarming. The UN official described the rate of forced displacement there as unlike anything seen in decades, with Palestinians being pushed from their homes at a pace that has drawn serious concern from international observers.

The UN human rights office, OHCHR, shared these findings publicly, drawing attention to what it described as ongoing destruction and encroachment by settlers in the region. Civilians in both Gaza and the West Bank continue to face dangerous and unstable conditions despite the ceasefire agreement.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Palestinians being displaced from their homes in the West Bank, and for all civilians caught in ongoing violence

events.title: Armed Conflict in United Arab Emirates — United Arab Emirates
events.body:
A drone struck near a nuclear power plant in the United Arab Emirates, prompting concern at the highest levels of the United Nations. The head of the UN's nuclear watchdog responded the following day by calling attention to the importance of protecting nuclear sites during wartime.

The UN official did not specify who carried out the strike or what damage, if any, resulted. The statement focused on the broader principle that nuclear facilities must be kept safe regardless of surrounding conflict.

Strikes near nuclear plants raise serious concerns for surrounding communities, as any damage to such a facility could have consequences well beyond the immediate area.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Communities in the United Arab Emirates facing risk after a drone strike near a nuclear power plant

events.title: Armed Conflict in Ukraine
events.body:
Civilians and humanitarian workers in Ukraine continue to face serious danger as fighting goes on across the country, according to the UN's humanitarian coordination office, OCHA. The agency has raised concern about the ongoing risk to people trying to survive or deliver aid in war-affected areas.

In Haiti, the UN report also flagged violence against children as a growing crisis. Children there are being caught up in gang-related and broader social violence that continues to destabilize communities across the country.

Separately, the report touched on efforts in Africa to provide identification documents to refugees — a step that can help displaced people access services, protection, and legal recognition in the countries where they have sought safety.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Civilians and humanitarian workers across Ukraine facing ongoing danger amid active armed conflict

events.title: Civil Unrest in Kenya 05/18/2026 — Nairobi
events.body:
At least four people were killed and more than 30 injured in Kenya during nationwide protests over sharply rising fuel prices. Police fired tear gas at crowds who blocked major roads with burning tyres and threw stones. At least 348 people were arrested on Monday, according to the interior minister, who said "criminal elements" had been involved in targeting property.

The unrest began with a public transport strike called by bus operators, bringing large cities including the capital and Mombasa to a near standstill. Commuters were forced to walk long distances to work and school. Transport unions are demanding the government reverse a fuel price hike of up to 23.5 percent last week, which followed a similar rise the month before. Higher fuel costs are also pushing up food and basic goods prices.

A Kenyan rights group condemned the use of lethal force by law enforcement. Kenya has seen repeated deadly protests in recent years, including a 2024 crackdown on tax protests that killed at least 60 people according to rights groups.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Kenyan civilians killed and hundreds arrested amid nationwide protests over soaring fuel and food prices

events.title: Civil Unrest in Bolivia 05/18/2026 — La Paz
events.body:
Thousands of supporters of Bolivia's former president marched into La Paz on Monday after a six-day trek through the Andes, clashing with riot police upon arrival. Protesters carried dynamite sticks and slingshots, and blasts were heard in the city center. Security forces responded with tear gas as demonstrators called on President Rodrigo Paz to resign, just six months into his term.

The protests are part of a broader movement now in its third week, driven by what officials describe as Bolivia's worst economic crisis in 40 years. Road blockades on key highways have stranded thousands of trucks, causing shortages of food, fuel, and medical supplies in La Paz and other cities. At least 90 people had been arrested as of Monday.

The government says it has reached agreements with striking miners and teachers, but many other groups remain in the streets. Argentina announced a weeklong airlift to help ease shortages at Bolivia's request.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Bolivian civilians amid violent protests, supply shortages, and a deepening economic crisis affecting cities nationwide

events.title: Civil Unrest in Venezuela — Venezuela
events.body:
Protesters in Venezuela clashed with police at a rally calling for the release of political prisoners. The demonstration followed the death of a woman whose son had been held in custody — she died ten days after learning he had died while imprisoned.

The son had been reported missing before his death in custody was confirmed to the family. His mother's passing sparked public outcry and brought demonstrators into the streets to demand accountability and the release of others held under similar circumstances.

The clash with police marks renewed public anger over the treatment of political detainees in the country. Families of those imprisoned continue to face uncertainty about the whereabouts and condition of their loved ones.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Families in Venezuela grieving and demanding justice amid ongoing political detentions and civil unrest

events.title: Armed Conflict in Israel — Gaza City
events.body:
A senior Hamas military figure was reportedly killed in an Israeli airstrike on Gaza City, according to a Hamas official speaking privately to journalists. Israeli officials had announced the previous day that strikes were carried out targeting the commander, saying he was responsible for attacks on Israeli civilians and soldiers, though they stopped short of confirming his death. Hamas made no formal public statement.

At least seven people were killed and around 50 injured in strikes that hit an apartment building and a nearby vehicle. Among the dead were three women and a child. It was not confirmed whether the commander died in that specific strike.

Residents described a city gripped by fear, with shopkeepers closing their stalls and families gathering to share news. Medical workers said hospitals are overwhelmed and operating with rationed supplies. Street vendors reported people trading goods for basic necessities like baby formula rather than buying for normal daily life.
events.source_title: jowhar.com
events.prayer: civilians in Gaza living through ongoing strikes, and for medical workers treating the wounded with limited supplies

events.title: Civil Unrest in Iran — Oromiyeh
events.body:
Since a military strike on Iran on February 28, the United Nations says it has verified the execution of at least 32 political prisoners. Human rights group Amnesty International reports that 45 executions on politically motivated charges have taken place in Iran across 2025 so far — a sharp increase compared to the same period last year. The UN's Human Rights Office has warned that the death penalty is increasingly being used to silence dissent, and expressed concern that some executions may be happening in secret.

Among those executed was a man who had been held at a prison in western Iran since his arrest during nationwide protests in 2022. He maintained his innocence and said his confession had been obtained through torture. A 21-year-old athlete from Isfahan was also put to death after being convicted of charges related to January 2025 protests, though he was not accused of any lethal offense — a threshold required under international law for use of the death penalty.

Residents across Iran have reported near-daily announcements of executions. Iran carried out 2,159 executions in 2024, the highest number since 1989, and the UN fears the 2025 figure could be higher still.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Dozens of political prisoners in Iran executed amid fears of secret killings and rising use of the death penalty to silence dissent

events.title: Armed Conflict in Iran 05/18/2026 — southern Lebanon
events.body:
On day 80 of the conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, tensions remain high across the region. The US president publicly warned Iran that time is running out to reach a deal, while reports suggest the US and Israel may be considering strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure. Iran's defense ministry responded by saying its military is fully prepared to respond to any attack.

In Lebanon, Israeli strikes continued across the south even as a ceasefire extension was announced, raising questions about what the agreement actually covers on the ground.

Separately, Saudi Arabia said it intercepted three drones, one day after a drone struck a nuclear energy plant in the United Arab Emirates. The back-to-back incidents point to a conflict that is no longer contained to one border, with civilian infrastructure and energy facilities now in the line of fire across multiple countries.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians across Lebanon enduring continued strikes amid an uncertain ceasefire agreement

events.title: Armed Conflict in Mali 05/18/2025 — San
events.body:
At least 10 civilians were killed when Mali's military carried out drone strikes on a group of people preparing for a traditional wedding in the Tene locality, in the central region of San. A procession of motorbikes heading to the celebration appeared to have drawn the attention of the drones. A resident described the loss as immense sorrow, saying "what was supposed to be a moment of joy in the village turned into immense sorrow."

The strikes came during a serious security crisis that erupted in late April, when al-Qaeda-linked fighters and Tuareg separatist rebels launched a coordinated offensive, killing the country's defense minister and capturing towns in the north. The rebel alliance has since imposed a blockade on the capital, Bamako.

A separate attack on May 7 killed at least 30 people in the Mopti region. Mali has faced ongoing unrest since 2012, and the conflict has intensified significantly in recent weeks, leaving civilians in central and northern areas increasingly exposed to violence from multiple armed groups.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Mali killed in drone strikes during a traditional wedding celebration amid escalating armed conflict

events.title: Armed Conflict in Nigeria 05/18/2025 — northeastern Nigeria
events.body:
The United States military carried out additional air strikes against fighters linked to the Islamic State in northeastern Nigeria on Sunday, working alongside Nigerian forces. U.S. Africa Command confirmed no American or Nigerian forces were hurt in the strikes. Officials said the operation reduced the group's ability to plan future attacks.

The strikes came two days after both governments announced the killing of a senior Islamic State leader in the Lake Chad Basin, described as the group's second-in-command, along with several others at his compound. Analysts say his death leaves a significant gap in the group's leadership and funding networks.

Dozens of U.S. troops have been stationed in Nigeria in recent months to provide intelligence and technical support, though Nigerian officials say American soldiers are not playing a direct combat role. U.S. forces also carried out strikes in northwestern Nigeria last Christmas, suggesting this military cooperation has been ongoing for some time.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians and communities in northeastern Nigeria amid ongoing airstrikes targeting armed extremist fighters

events.title: Armed Conflict in Ukraine 05/18/2026 — Odesa
events.body:
Russian drones struck two civilian cargo ships in the Black Sea near Ukraine's Odesa region on Monday. One of the vessels, a Chinese-owned cargo ship with a Chinese crew, was partially charred after being hit but continued on its way to port. No crew members were injured. The other ship was sailing under Guinea-Bissau's flag.

Odesa is a critical port for Ukraine's agricultural exports and has been targeted repeatedly since Russia invaded in February 2022. Ukrainian officials noted that the struck Chinese vessel was entering port empty, intending to load iron ore concentrate.

In the same overnight attack, Ukrainian officials reported that Russian forces launched 524 drones and 22 missiles — including ballistic and cruise missiles — across Ukrainian territory. The attack on the Chinese-owned ship occurred one day before Russian leadership was set to travel to Beijing for meetings with Chinese officials.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilian cargo ships and maritime trade routes in Ukraine under repeated drone and missile strikes

events.title: Civil Unrest in Israel — Jerusalem Old City
events.body:
Tens of thousands of Israeli celebrants marched through Jerusalem's Old City to mark the anniversary of Israel's capture of the area during the 1967 Arab-Israeli war. The annual event is a recurring source of tension in the city.

The march is frequently accompanied by racist chants and violence directed at Palestinian residents and their businesses, according to the BBC correspondent who observed the event on the ground. Israeli annexation of East Jerusalem is not recognized by many of its allies, including the United Kingdom.

The Old City and surrounding areas remain deeply contested, and the march passes through neighborhoods where Palestinian families live and work. The event draws large crowds each year and has consistently sparked confrontations between marchers and local residents.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Palestinian residents in Israel facing harassment and violence during annual nationalist march through contested areas

events.title: Armed Conflict in Nigeria 05/16/2025 — Mussa, Borno state
events.body:
Gunmen stormed three schools in Mussa, a town in Borno state in north-eastern Nigeria, kidnapping more than 50 children. Most of those taken are between two and five years old. Witnesses say the attackers arrived on motorcycles shortly after a military patrol left the area, fired into the air to scatter residents, and then used the children as human shields while fleeing — preventing security forces from opening fire.

No group has claimed responsibility. Some observers say the attack resembles past operations by Boko Haram, which competes with a rival armed faction for control of the region. A senator from Borno South confirmed at least 42 children had been abducted from two of the schools; the total across all three is reported as more than 50.

Some families have fled Mussa out of fear, while many parents remain behind, waiting for news. Security forces are reported to be actively searching for the abductors. The community, already struggling economically, is urgently calling on the government to help bring the children home safely.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: More than 50 young children abducted from schools in northeastern Nigeria, families waiting in fear for their return

events.title: Armed Conflict in Israel — Kiryat Shmona
events.body:
Hezbollah has sharply increased its use of small, low-cost drones to strike Israeli soldiers and military equipment in southern Lebanon and northern Israel. An analysis of verified video footage shows attacks on armored vehicles, air defense systems, and a military border outpost near Kiryat Shmona. Israeli media reports at least four soldiers and one civilian have been killed in these strikes, with dozens more wounded.

What makes these drones especially difficult to stop is their use of fiber-optic cables instead of radio signals, which allows them to bypass Israel's electronic jamming systems. Security experts say Israel has so far been unable to develop effective countermeasures. Troops have been forced to move more carefully, reinforce positions with physical barriers, and increase local defense awareness.

The drones cost as little as $300–$500 each and can be assembled from commercially available parts, including components made with 3D printers — making them easy to produce and hard to trace. Residents near the border on both sides of the conflict face continued risk as these attacks show no sign of slowing.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Israeli soldiers and border communities in northern Israel under escalating low-cost drone attacks with no effective countermeasures yet

events.title: Armed Conflict in Russia — Moscow region
events.body:
Three people were killed and at least 16 others injured after a large-scale Ukrainian drone attack struck the Moscow region overnight. A woman died in Khimki, north of Moscow, where another person was trapped under rubble. Two more people were killed in the village of Pogorelki. Twelve people were hurt when drones hit an oil refinery on the edge of Moscow. Russia said its air defenses intercepted 556 drones in total. Drone debris also landed at Sheremetyevo, Moscow's busiest airport, though no injuries were reported there.

Ukraine's government said the strikes targeted oil facilities and a semiconductor plant in the Moscow region, as well as air defense systems at a military airfield in Crimea. Ukrainian officials described the targets as legitimate because they support Russia's war effort.

Meanwhile, Russian forces launched more than 30 drone and shelling attacks on Ukraine's Dnipropetrovsk region overnight, injuring eight people and damaging homes. A woman was also injured in a separate Russian drone strike in the Zaporizhzhia region. Earlier in the week, a Russian missile and drone attack on Kyiv killed 24 people.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Residents across Russia facing casualties and destruction after large-scale overnight drone strikes

events.title: Congo — Ituri
events.body:
The World Health Organization has declared an Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo a public health emergency of international concern. The outbreak is centered in the eastern Ituri province, where around 246 suspected cases and 80 deaths have been reported across three health zones, including the provincial capital Bunia and the gold-mining towns of Mongwalu and Rwampara. One case has also been confirmed in the capital Kinshasa. The current strain is caused by the Bundibugyo virus, for which no approved drugs or vaccines exist.

The outbreak has crossed borders. Two confirmed cases have been reported in neighboring Uganda, and a case has been confirmed in Goma. WHO officials warned the true number of infections is likely higher than reported, with significant risk of wider local and regional spread.

The WHO cited the ongoing security situation, high population movement, urban locations, and large numbers of informal health clinics as factors raising the risk. Neighboring countries are considered high risk due to trade and travel links.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Congolese communities facing a deadly Ebola outbreak with no approved treatment and risk of wider regional spread

events.title: Civil Unrest in Tunisia 05/17/2026 — Tunis
events.body:
Hundreds of people marched through Tunisia's capital to protest a deepening economic crisis and what demonstrators describe as an increasing crackdown on political dissent. The protesters voiced opposition to what they see as the erosion of freedoms gained after the 2011 revolution.

The country's president faces accusations of weakening the democratic institutions that emerged from that period. Political arrests have added to public frustration alongside the ongoing economic difficulties.

The demonstrations reflect growing tension between the government and those who fear a reversal of the political gains made over the past decade. Ordinary Tunisians continue to feel the pressure of both financial hardship and a shrinking space for public opposition.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Tunisian citizens amid deepening economic hardship and rising restrictions on political freedoms

events.title: Armed Conflict in Iran 05/17/2026 — Zawtar al-Sharqiyah
events.body:
Iran announced it will soon reveal a plan to charge tolls on traffic passing through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global shipping. A senior Iranian official stated the country will no longer allow military equipment from "enemy" nations to pass through the strait. The United States president warned Iran it would face a "very bad time" if a peace deal is not reached soon.

Separately, Israeli airstrikes continued in southern Lebanon, with an attack reported on the town of Zawtar al-Sharqiyah. Israel's bombardment of the region has been described as relentless, raising ongoing concerns for civilians living in affected communities.

The two situations together reflect a wider pattern of tension across the Middle East, with pressure building both on land in Lebanon and over contested waters at the Strait of Hormuz.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Lebanon enduring ongoing Israeli airstrikes amid escalating regional tensions

events.title: Armed Conflict in Russia 05/17/2026 — Moscow region
events.body:
Ukraine launched what Russian officials called one of the largest drone attacks of the war, sending waves of drones toward Moscow and other regions overnight. At least five people were killed — including a foreign worker and a woman whose home was struck in Khimki, north of Moscow, and two men in the Mytishchi district. One more person died in Belgorod, near the Ukrainian border. Russia's defense ministry said it intercepted 556 drones across 14 regions, as well as over annexed Crimea and nearby seas. Debris from the attacks fell on the grounds of Moscow's largest airport, though no damage was reported there.

Ukraine's security service said its forces struck an oil refinery and two oil-pumping stations in the Moscow region. Ukrainian officials said the attacks were a direct response to ongoing Russian strikes on Ukrainian cities.

Meanwhile, Russian attacks continued inside Ukraine. In the Kharkiv region, 15 settlements were targeted over 24 hours, wounding seven people. In the Kherson region, a man was killed when a Russian drone dropped explosives on a village.
events.source_title: Al Jazeera – Breaking News, World News and Video from Al Jazeera
events.prayer: Civilians in Russia and Ukraine caught in escalating large-scale drone and missile exchanges across both countries

events.title: Armed Conflict in Iran 02/28/2026 — Minab
events.body:
A senior US military commander told a Senate hearing this week that his command has not investigated widely reported claims that US airstrikes have hit schools and hospitals across Iran. The head of US Central Command said there is "no indication" that 22 schools were struck, despite reports from the Iranian Red Crescent Society claiming that attacks on universities and schools have killed at least 60 students and 10 staff members.

The commander acknowledged only one active civilian casualty investigation out of more than 13,000 munitions used in strikes on Iran. That investigation relates to a February 28 strike on a girls' elementary school in Minab, where reports say 156 students and staff were killed and 95 others wounded. US officials initially denied responsibility, but journalistic investigations and a preliminary Pentagon review have pointed to US involvement.

A senator pressed the commander repeatedly on why the allegations had not been investigated, especially given his stated commitment to minimizing civilian harm. The commander offered no clear explanation for the lack of inquiries.
events.source_title: antiwar.com
events.prayer: Students, teachers, and civilians in Iran killed and wounded amid ongoing airstrikes targeting populated areas including schools

events.title: Displacement in Sudan — Sudan
events.body:
Nearly 20 million people across Sudan are facing acute hunger, according to a warning issued by United Nations agencies. More than 800,000 children are at risk of severe malnutrition this year alone. Parts of the country are being described as edging closer to famine conditions.

The crisis is being driven by ongoing civil war, the mass displacement of civilians, and the collapse of both food supply and health systems. What was already one of the world's worst humanitarian situations continues to worsen as fighting disrupts the ability of aid organizations to respond.

Families who have fled their homes face the greatest danger, with little access to food, clean water, or medical care. The combination of conflict and hunger is pushing many communities past what they can survive without outside help.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Nearly 20 million people in Sudan facing acute hunger, mass displacement, and deepening famine conditions amid civil war

events.title: Armed Conflict in Afghanistan 03/16/2026 — Kabul
events.body:
At least 269 people were killed on March 16, 2026, when an airstrike hit the Omid Drug Rehabilitation Hospital in Kabul. Pakistan told the BBC it had targeted "military and terrorist infrastructure," but the strike hit a facility where civilians were receiving treatment for drug addiction.

The BBC spoke with families of more than 30 victims, including relatives of patients in recovery and hospital staff, all of whom rejected Pakistan's account of the strike. The United Nations was granted access to the site and, along with BBC journalists on the ground in the hours after the attack, confirmed that civilians undergoing treatment were killed.

The full toll on those inside the facility — patients, staff, and others — remains one of the deadliest single strikes on Afghan soil in recent years, leaving many families without answers about why their loved ones were targeted.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Hundreds of civilians in Afghanistan killed when an airstrike destroyed a drug rehabilitation hospital in Kabul

events.title: Armed Conflict in Ukraine 05/16/2025 — Kyiv
events.body:
A Russian cruise missile struck a nine-storey apartment building in southeastern Kyiv, killing 24 people including three girls aged 12 and 15. Rescue workers spent 28 hours searching the rubble before ending their operation. Among those killed were postal workers, a former hockey player, a kindergarten teacher, and an English teacher. A 12-year-old girl who lost her father earlier in the war also died, along with her older sister. Kyiv held a day of mourning, and flowers were laid at the site.

On the same day, Russian officials reported that Ukrainian drone strikes hit the city of Ryazan, southeast of Moscow, killing four people including a child and wounding 28 others. A Ukrainian drone commander said the attack targeted Ryazan's oil refinery, one of Russia's largest.

Also on Friday, Russia and Ukraine exchanged 205 prisoners of war each, the first stage of a planned 1,000-person swap brokered by the United States and United Arab Emirates. The exchange followed a three-day ceasefire earlier in May that both sides said was repeatedly violated, and which ended with a wave of large-scale Russian strikes across Ukraine.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians in Ukraine grieving after a deadly missile strike on a residential apartment building killed 24 people

events.title: Armed Conflict in Israel — Gaza City
events.body:
An Israeli air strike on a residential building in central Gaza City killed a senior Hamas military commander on Friday, according to Hamas and Israeli officials. Eyewitnesses told the BBC that three missiles struck the apartment block from two directions, sparking a large fire. Rescue teams struggled to reach the wounded. A second strike hit a car about a kilometre and a half away, killing three people. Witnesses said the vehicle may have been carrying the wounded commander as he fled.

A ceasefire between Israel and Hamas came into effect on 10 October, but Israel has continued to carry out strikes across Gaza since then. Israel says it has the right to target Hamas members, while Hamas accuses Israel of violating the ceasefire and attacking civilians. The two sides remain at a standoff over disarmament, with Hamas reactivating its police force and reasserting control in parts of Gaza.

Since the ceasefire began, 857 people have been killed in Gaza, according to the Hamas-run health ministry. Broader peace talks remain deadlocked.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: civilians living near military targets in Gaza City, and for progress in stalled peace efforts

events.title: Armed Conflict in Lebanon — Harouf
events.body:
An Israeli air strike on the town of Harouf in southern Lebanon killed six people, including three paramedics, according to Lebanon's health ministry. A fourth paramedic was critically injured when a civil defence centre was hit. Days earlier, on Wednesday, air strikes killed 22 people across the south, including eight children.

Despite a ceasefire announced by US President Donald Trump in April, both sides have continued trading fire almost daily. The US announced a 45-day extension of the truce after two days of negotiations in Washington, with a military track of talks set to begin at the Pentagon on May 29.

More than one million people — roughly one in five Lebanese — have been displaced since the conflict began, most from the south, the Bekaa Valley, and southern Beirut. At least 2,896 people have been killed in Lebanon since fighting started, according to the health ministry.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians and paramedics in Lebanon killed amid ongoing strikes despite a fragile ceasefire

events.title: Armed Conflict in Nigeria — Metele, Borno state
events.body:
Nigeria and the United States carried out a joint operation that killed a senior Islamic State (IS) leader, along with several of his associates, at a compound in Metele, Borno state, in northeastern Nigeria. The operation began shortly after midnight on a Saturday, following months of intelligence gathering. Officials said no casualties were suffered during the strike.

The man killed was described by US officials as among the most senior figures in the global IS hierarchy and was linked to attacks on civilians across West Africa and the Sahel, including the 2018 kidnapping of more than 100 schoolgirls in Dapchi, Nigeria. He had previously been a senior commander within Boko Haram before aligning with IS.

The Lake Chad Basin, where the strike took place, has long been a stronghold for extremist groups. IS now carries out roughly 90% of its attacks in sub-Saharan Africa, with its Nigeria-based branch considered the most active. Both governments called the operation a sign of growing military cooperation between the two countries.
events.source_title: BBC News
events.prayer: Civilians across Nigeria facing continued extremist violence from active Islamic State networks in the Lake Chad region

events.title: Displacement in Somalia — Somalia
events.body:
United Nations aid teams warned on Friday that Somalia faces a real risk of famine, with at least six million people going days at a time without enough food. Nearly two million of those affected are young children described as being at high risk of illness or death.

The warning came as the fallout from the conflict in the Middle East continues to affect humanitarian funding and aid delivery in other parts of the world. Somalia has long struggled with food insecurity, and the current situation appears to be worsening.

With millions of people — including large numbers of vulnerable children — unable to meet basic food needs, the humanitarian situation remains severe. The UN's alert signals that without a significant response, conditions on the ground could deteriorate into full famine.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Six million people in Somalia, including nearly two million young children, facing severe hunger and potential famine

events.title: Armed Conflict in Israel — Gaza, West Bank, Lebanon
events.body:
Civilians across Gaza, the West Bank, and Lebanon are facing deepening hardship as military operations continue and humanitarian conditions worsen, according to UN agencies. Displacement is ongoing, and aid efforts are being strained by the scale of need.

UN reports indicate that the conflict is making it increasingly difficult for relief organizations to reach those who need help most. Food, medical care, and basic services remain limited across the affected areas.

The situation is described as a growing humanitarian crisis, with no immediate signs of relief for those caught in the middle of the fighting.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: civilians in Gaza, the West Bank, and Lebanon who are caught in ongoing violence and struggling to access food, medical care, and safe shelter

events.title: Displacement in Congo — eastern Congo
events.body:
Ongoing fighting in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo has forced millions of people from their homes, including large numbers of children whose education has been disrupted by the conflict.

In response, new schools have been established to serve displaced children in the region. These efforts aim to give young people a sense of normalcy and hope amid the instability, allowing them to continue learning even after being uprooted from their communities.

Despite the difficult conditions, children in these schools are expressing ambitions for their futures, a sign that access to education is making a meaningful difference in their lives. For families scattered by war, schools are becoming one of the few stable anchors available to children growing up in displacement.
events.source_title: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
events.prayer: Millions displaced in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, including children losing access to stable education amid ongoing conflict
